@dabeaz 
> Curio is an expert tool for expert developers solving a hard problem (concurrency, network programming, etc.).

I think this might be at the core of why we're at such cross-purposes here... from conversations so far, I get the impression that the experts you have in mind are folks (like yourself?) whose main interest is in messing about with the guts of event loops and experimenting with all the little details?

OTOH, when I think of expert developers, the image in my mind -- the heroes I try to emulate -- are people with brains the size of planets who spend that energy on building complex systems out of simple, straightforward code that's obviously correct, and who appreciate APIs that make that easier to do.

Right now, the only way to write an obviously correct I/O loop in curio is to put an `await curio.sleep(0)` in it. The emphasis here is on the word "obvious": a loop that doesn't have `sleep(0)` might be correct, but the only way to know is to do a detailed analysis of the surrounding code, client, expected deployment conditions, etc. If I were writing a code review checklist for a project using curio, one of the entries I'd have to put in is: "For each I/O loop: is there either (a) a call to `curio.sleep(0)` or (b) an analysis of why this is unnecessary?" Not the kind of thing you need when you're trying to build something complex and curio is just the boring networking layer.

Also, it should hopefully go without saying, but just in case: these are both totally valid approaches! Just... it's not clear how compatible they are. I guess we will probably continue to push-and-pull on this going forward.

@glyph 
> the ability to do scheduling at all is only the first step to doing some kind of fair scheduling.

I actually went down the rabbit hole reading about fair scheduling last night, and it looks like it'd actually be super easy to implement in curio :-). I'm trying to resist though, since there's really no use until we have more complex apps to play with... but yeah, even a smart scheduler can't do anything unless the tasks have schedule points in them.

@dabeaz 
> The code is "eager", but if you modified it to wait for reading first (i.e., calling _read_wait()), I'm not sure it would work at all.

Heh, good point! As a technical matter, though, it's easily solvable -- my suggestion is that `recv` should guarantee that it acts as a schedule point, not that it necessary calls `_read_wait()`. So e.g. this would work fine:

```python
async def recv(self, maxsize, flags=0):
    while True:
        try:
            result = self._socket_recv(maxsize, flags)
            await _sleep(0, False)
            return result
        except WantRead:
            await _read_wait(self._fileno)
        except WantWrite:
            await _write_wait(self._fileno)
```
One could (should) make that prettier and smarter, but it accomplishes the basic requirement.

Oh, and speaking of removing `writable`, I'm actually considering one approach for doing that in trio here: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/371

But I doubt Dave will like it :-)

Hm, I'm not and expert neither in python bytecode hacking nor in GIL, but here is my reasoning. We now that in cpython GIL switches only between bytecode instructions, so an operation is considered atomic in python if it translates to a single bytecode instruction ([I take this post a reference](http://blog.qqrs.us/blog/2016/05/01/which-python-operations-are-atomic/)). Furthermore, if we disassemble the current `local.Local.__setattr__`:
```python
>>> from curio import local
>>> import dis
>>> dis.dis(local.Local.__setattr__)
120           0 LOAD_FAST                2 (value)
              3 LOAD_GLOBAL              0 (_local_dict)
              6 LOAD_FAST                0 (self)
              9 CALL_FUNCTION            1 (1 positional, 0 keyword pair) # this
             12 LOAD_FAST                1 (name)
             15 STORE_SUBSCR  # and this
             16 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             19 RETURN_VALUE
>>>
```
we can see that there are to instructions `CALL_FUNCTION` and `STORE_SUBSCR`, that _**might**_ cause and inconsistency.

Just wondering... having reviewed the code with the new selector class, it seems like it wakes all waiting tasks whenever there is a matching I/O event.   This could create a real interesting sort of thundering herd problem.  For example, maybe the first task only does a partial read of the data (because of the maxsize on recv).  But then the 2nd task picks up where it left off and reads any remaining data on the socket.  The 2nd task would do this without any sort of blocking since the data would just be sitting there, ready to be read.    Meh.  I don't know.  Maybe it's better to limit this to one task reading, one task writing, per file descriptor.

Thanks for the report @rtkmrussell. The way sideloading is currently implemented is a bit terrible, I've been meaning to refactor it for a while now. 

What you tried is perfectly logical but not supported by the current implementation. The way it works is sideloads are defined per endpoint, for example - https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/endpoint.py#L468. 

I must have missed the requests sideload as they are not defined for the requests endpoint - https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/endpoint.py#L441.

Thankfully, you should be able to fix this by adding the sideload as follows:

```python
zenpy_client.requests.append_sideload('users')
```

From this point forward users should be sideloaded.

Let me know if this doesn't work or you have any questions.

Having quickly glanced at your branch I can tell you that callers (at least Boto3) assume the end of file was reached if `read()` returns less than requested.

The way I'm using it now, `writelines()` creates a file-like wrapper around the iterator, which looks like this:
```
class StreamingFile(object):
    """A file object wrapped around an iterator / data stream."""

    def __init__(self, data_stream):
        """Intialise the object with the data stream."""
        self.data_stream = data_stream
        self.buffer = ''

    def read(self, size=float('inf')):
        """Read bytes from an iterator."""
        while len(self.buffer) < size:
            try:
                self.buffer += self.data_stream.next()
            except StopIteration:
                break

        sized_chunk = self.buffer[:size]
        self.buffer = self.buffer[size:]
        return sized_chunk
```

I ran into an issue when trying to add `fit_param` support for `StackingCVClassifier`. It issue arises when each CV fold is being trained on.

https://github.com/rasbt/mlxtend/blob/5735e00afcf83e014b90a6726de8cf91354406eb/mlxtend/classifier/stacking_cv_classification.py#L168-L175

Some classifier fit parameters are arrays that have a value for each sample being trained on (e.g. the `sample_weight` parameter for `RandomForestClassifier`). This case is pretty straight-forward because the `sample_weight` array can be indexed using the same `train_index` array as the training data. 

However, it's less clear how to deal with fit parameters that aren't of shape `(n_samples,)`. For example, `SGDClassifier` has `coef_init` and `intercept_init` fit parameters that are of shape `(n_classes, n_features)` and `(n_classes,)`, respectively. I guess one could check if a fit pararmeter is an array of shape `(n_samples,)`, and if so index it with the `train_index` array. But this seems a little hack-ish to me :confused: I can already think of a couple of edge cases that would lead to problems. 

Any suggestions on how to get around this issue?

Version 2 by defaults read the server schema. So if you open and close the connection it will perform a search on the schema, that can takes a relatively long time. This is because RFC states that the client must be aware of the server schema and must perform checks before sending data. You can try to define the server with get_info=NONE then execution  time should be similar to version 1.4.

I was thinking of caching the schema but this should not be permitted by the RFC.

Let me know if you get faster with get_info set to NONE.

Hi @FinQuest,

The whole block of code starting at 1062 in `core.py` was introduced to address the issue where the commissions are ignored when determining the quantities. This is initially how it worked, but it essentially created some sort of implicit leverage. For small commissions, it wasn't a huge deal, but it could throw off the performance figures & weights in the case of large commissions.

To address this, I had to basically figure out a way to solve for the quantity including the costs of commissions. Since the commission function can be non-linear, this is a non-trivial problem. To be honest, I'm not too happy with the current solution, but I could not think of anything more robust at the time (definitely open to suggestions here!). That's why I just slowly decrement the quantity by 1 unit at a time and re-evaluate the commission to see if the capital allocated is now sufficient to cover the quantity desired + commissions. Obviously, this is not a very elegant solution, and the unit decrease doesn't make that much sense in a `interger_positions=False` scenario. However, short of having some kind of check for linearity, I'm not sure how to get around this issue. 

I hope this makes sense and I am open to ideas if you have any. 

Cheers,
Phil

[tʃˡ] and [dʒˡ] should work, but they must be converted to [t͡ʃˡ] and [d͡ʒˡ] to bring them into conformity with IPA/Panphon. Also, the "Lateral Release" rule must be relaxed; `delrelː "-"` must be removed. If `contː "-"` is removed, then [sˡ ʃˡ] should also work. I find it a bit awkward to talk about release on frictives, or to speak of fricatives as delayed release. Otherwise, though, they might collide with lateral fricatives.

The other symbols all have problems that make them difficult to include in the current scheme. For example, from the standpoint of distinctive features, it is not clear how [z̥] is different from [s], or how [s̬] is different from [z]. The rhotic diacritic from IPA is difficult to characterize in terms of features because rhoticity does not have a consistent phonetic definition. [p̪] and [b̪] must be labiodental stops. I suppose they could be both [+lab] and [+cor], but since they only occur in disordered speech, it's hard to know where to place them.

The root problem is that the feature system I am using (in the SPE tradition) is made to capture the contrasts among sounds, across languages, in non-disordered speech. Since the (non-contrastive) patterns in disordered speech show differences that are never contrastive in the languages of the world, such a feature system is inherently ill-suited for characterizing disordered speech.

At one time, feature systems were modular in Panphon, so it was relatively easy to select different `ipa_bases.csv` and `diacritic_definitions.yml`. If you would like to create support for a feature system that works better on disordered speech, I would be willing to reintroduce a modular architecture for feature systems in Panphon.

Few things here:
 - For compatibility with more platforms and/or python versions, consider `open(filepath, "rb")` to explicitly open the PDF file in binary mode.
 - I added `__getitem__` later, so you would just need a newer version for that.

Sorry for all the [changes](/jalan/pdftotext/blob/master/CHANGES.md), but I made sure to document them and to update the version number accordingly. This should do what you want:

```
import pdftotext

def get_text(filepath, page=None):
    with open(filepath, "rb") as f:
        pdf = pdftotext.PDF(f)
    if page is not None:
        text = pdf[page]
    else:
        text = "\n\n".join(pdf)
    return text
```

The following  components are the main changes needed for the netmiko library to be compatible with Python 2 and Python 3. 

- Using the print() function instead of the print statement
```
if debug:
  print("In set_base_prompt")
```
- Changing relative import statements to absolute
```
from netmiko.cisco.cisco_ios_ssh import CiscoIosSSH
```
or
```
from . cisco_ios_ssh import CiscoIosSSH
```
instead of 

```
from  cisco_ios_ssh import CiscoIosSSH
```

- Using the unicode 'sandwich' pattern where input bytes (output from SSH sessions, files read from disk, etc) are decoded to unicode as soon as possible. Unicode strings are carried throughout the program, and if need to output, encoded back to bytes right before output. 
  - All string manipulation code will not be affected as long as all input bytes are decoded to unicode right away.
  - utf-8 encoding should be used
``` 
output = self.remote_conn.recv(MAX_BUFFER).decode('utf-8')
```
- For opening files, the  `io` module's `open()` method should be used, as it has an `encoding` argument just like the builtin `open` function in Python 3. This will take care of any input bytes read from disk entering the program.

```
import io
with io.open(config_file, encoding='utf-8') as cfg_file:
    return self.send_config_set(cfg_file, **kwargs)
```

- ANSI ESC sequences currently using `chr(27)` will continue working under Python3 without modification, as ASCII values map directly to the same values in Unicode code points.

These are the main factors that would need to change in order to be compatible with Python3.

> Can't you just check if content is `None` and then pass in `accept_untrusted_content=True` ?

Well, no, not at the moment, given #24. I certainly think making the default values of `content` and `content_type` some kind of non-`None` `EmptyValue`, [as you suggest](https://github.com/kumar303/mohawk/issues/24#issuecomment-179517062), would help some. But thus my question about verbosity -- do to it right, you'd have to do something like this:

```python
content = request.body or ''
content_type = request.META.get('CONTENT_TYPE', '')
accept_untrusted_content = (request.method == 'GET' and not content and not content_type)
receiver = Receiver(lookup_hawk_credentials,
                    auth_header,
                    url,
                    request.method,
                    content=content,
                    content_type=content_type,
                    accept_untrusted_content=accept_untrusted_content)
```

Not exactly compact, is it?

> But anyway, this is still dangerous because an attacker could possibly capture / alter a request and make the body empty to bypass the checks -- this may be harmless or it may do damage depending on how you process the request.

This is what timestamps and nonces are for, not content hashing. Content hashing is for *content*. If there's no content, and there isn't any *expected* content (GET requests), the hash check is *superfluous*. It should be easy to skip in this case (and *only* this case), and right now it's not.

Like I said: if you're trying to avoid verbosity, this isn't working. If you're trying to avoid misconfiguration, this isn't working, but in a different way.

> ...if you have full control of your clients...

You don't. That's really kind of the point of Hawk in general, isn't it? More specifically, though, the [official browser code](https://github.com/hueniverse/hawk/blob/master/lib/browser.js) doesn't hash by default, and, say, Postman [still doesn't hash](https://github.com/postmanlabs/postman-app-support/issues/2458) at all. I totally understand wanting more secure defaults, but right now it takes careful consideration and fairly specific knowledge of the internals to make a *very* common case work (GET, no content, no hash) without opening bigger holes (skipping checks in general). Which is not exactly the intended outcome of the options as currently implemented.

The reason here is hinted at in the comment about `pop()`, but I guess we never got around to fixing it. `__delitem__` and `pop` don't have an efficient way to scan the deque to remove the cached value, so it just... doesn't. For the case of duplicate values, as tested here, a similar issue is happening, where we don't know if the same value was reinserted later. A dict of counts might fix this. I'll have to think about it a bit more :)

From what I can turn up, OS X does not recognize the AltGr key separately from the regular Alt key. There are two possible solutions:

1. Remove the AltGr key from the all_modifiers list altogether on OS X.
-   Causes an exception when running cross-platform code that hooks the AltGr key.
2. Or, map the AltGr key to the regular Alt key on OS X.
-   This could cause unexpected behavior when running cross-platform code that hooks the AltGr key.

I think option 1 is the clearest for developers, and alerts them to the need to redesign AltGr-dependent code to run on OS X. @boppreh, official ruling?

This makes sense when looking at https://github.com/cmbruns/pyopenvr/blob/master/src/openvr/__init__.py#L3633, it expects the last parameter to be an uint.

So the current code will probably keep that size at 0, making the native code not fill the struct at all. This was the case with `getControllerState` when I was looking at it.

Considering the comment in C# binding say "were originally mis-compiled", if you are using one of these mis-compiled versions, this fix may not be enough and if that's the case you'll need to do some manual unpacking like is done in the C# bindings.

Some observations:
1. nodeenv must be installed in the virtual environment you want to use. It cannot exist only in the base Python environment that the virtualenv is part of.
2. The virtual environment must have nodeenv installed before activation. nodeenv that has been installed into an already-activated environment will not work until the environment is deactivated and reactivated again.

How to make it work:
1. Create your virtualenv.
2. Install nodeenv.
3. Deactivate your virtualenv.
4. Activate your virtualenv.
5. `nodeenv -p`

This is as intended. [`discord.Client`](https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#client) does not have a `command` attribute.

What you're probably looking for is `discord.ext.commands.Bot`, a subclass of `discord.Client` in the commands extension: https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/1863a1c6636f53592519320a173ec9573c090c0b/discord/ext/commands/bot.py#L146 It does have a `command` attribute (inherited from `discord.ext.commands.GroupMixin`, which it also subclasses), which can be used as a decorator to add commands: https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/1863a1c6636f53592519320a173ec9573c090c0b/discord/ext/commands/core.py#L568-L577 For the rewrite branch, see https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/rewrite/ext/commands/api.html#bot and https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/rewrite/ext/commands/api.html#discord.ext.commands.GroupMixin.

Any reference to a "command" is likely a part of the commands extension.
Assuming you're trying to use the `command` attribute as a decorator to add a command, you need to use a `Bot` object instead of a `Client` object.
As an example, see basic_bot.py in the examples folder.

For further help and questions regarding usage of the library, you should join either the [official discord.py server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ) or the [Discord API server](https://discord.gg/discord-api), as the README recommends.
Also, in the future, when requesting help with an error, you need to include the full traceback, not just the exception type and message, as well as any relevant code, so that anyone trying to help you can understand what you're trying to do and what's causing the error.

There are a few different issues with this idea.

1. The genetic value objects don't seem pickle-able.  (I've not been able to figure out a method to ensure that).  This matters for a variety of reasons.

2. I do not see a use case for getting anything other than genetic value, noise, and fitness out of these objects.  These values are already recorded as diploid metadata, and thus available to a recorder.

3. A recorder would not see the genetic value object anyways.   A recorder must be convertible to `std::function<void(const poptype &)>`.  So, the best case is you'd have access to the very last set of data. 

What is the use case for needing to see data other than what is already being recorded?  I usually consider the internal state to be the object's own business and that what really matters is its output.  When is that not the case?

Hi @iustin!

Yes, the `p.page` variable is stored as a zero-based index. All internal functions use a zero-based index. The [pager methods](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#methods-pager) (`pageAndSize`, `pageSet` and `pageSize`) all use a one-based index as this was expected to be used along with the user interface – which shows a one-based index to the user.

As a developer, you can change the `table.config.pager.page` and `table.config.pager.size` values directly using a zero-based index, then call the `$.tablesorter.pager.moveToPage(config, config.pager)` function.

Either way, I think it's too late to change this behavior now since it would not be backwards compatible, and I don't plan on creating a major revision with tablesorter. I will make this behavior consistent once I get around to working on [Abelt](https://github.com/tablesort/Abelt) again.

If anyone is interested in a use case for evaluations, I used a somewhat customized version of `boolean.py` with evaluations in one of my projects: https://github.com/gemerden/wildpath (`pip install wildpath`):

```python
# '|' is the OR operator

assert WildPath("start_time|end_time").get_in(agenda) == {"start_time": "10:00", "end_time": "11:00"}

# '&' is the AND operator

assert WildPath("start_*&*_time").get_in(agenda) == {"start_time": "10:00"}


# '!' is the NOT operator:

assert WildPath("!item?").get_in({"item1": "chair", "item2": "table", "count": 2}) == {"count": 2}

# parentheses can be used to indicate precedence:

assert WildPath("!(a|b)") != WildPath("!a|b")
```
`WildPath` supports paths, as in : `WildPath("route.stops.*.lat|lng").get_in(some_vehicle)` would give all geo-coordinates of the route of some vehicle.

Hmm, interesting. I think this is a result of the mp3 "file" ("part" in this context) not being loaded as a MediaPart object.

The mappings that control that are here:
https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/__init__.py#L27

As you'll see, there's no entry for `CT.MP3` or any other audio formats.

You can remedy that with this code, which must be run before you load your presentation:

```python
from pptx.opc.package import PartFactory
from pptx.parts.media import MediaPart

PartFactory.part_type_for.update(
    {
        'audio/mp3': MediaPart
    }
)
```

The MIME-type ('audio/mp3' in this example) will need to exactly match the type attributed to your MP3 file in the [Content_Types].xml part of your template package (.pptx file).

import ctypes

class POINT(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_ = [("x", ctypes.c_long), ("y", ctypes.c_long)]

# Left bounding corner
leftBound = POINT()

ctypes.windll.user32.SetCursorPos(-99999, -99999)
ctypes.windll.user32.GetCursorPos(ctypes.byref(leftBound))

print leftBound.x, leftBound.y

# Right bounding corner
rightBound = POINT()

ctypes.windll.user32.SetCursorPos(99999, -99999)
ctypes.windll.user32.GetCursorPos(ctypes.byref(rightBound))

print rightBound.x, rightBound.y

# Left bounding corner, bottom
leftBoundBot = POINT()

ctypes.windll.user32.SetCursorPos(-99999, 99999)
ctypes.windll.user32.GetCursorPos(ctypes.byref(leftBoundBot))

print leftBoundBot.x, leftBoundBot.y

# Right bounding corner, bottom
rightBoundBot = POINT()

ctypes.windll.user32.SetCursorPos(99999, 99999)
ctypes.windll.user32.GetCursorPos(ctypes.byref(rightBoundBot))

print rightBoundBot.x, rightBoundBot.y

# Reset cursor to middle of screen
ctypes.windll.user32.SetCursorPos(0, 0)

# Plot coordinates
a = [leftBound.x, 0, 0, rightBound.x, rightBoundBot.x,
     0, 0, leftBoundBot.x, leftBound.x]
b = [leftBound.y, leftBound.y, 0, 0, rightBoundBot.y,
     rightBoundBot.y, leftBoundBot.y, leftBoundBot.y, leftBound.y]
# a = [-each for each in a]
b = [-each for each in b]  # Correct for inverted axis locations for plotting
plt.plot(a, b, 'r')
plt.axis([-2000, 2000, -1200, 1200])
plt.show()
`

Re `to_HAP` -> `to_dict`. I'm a little concerned about this. `to_HAP` exists to create a representation of the characteristic that the HAP protocol understands. The fact that it's a `dict` is just an implementation detail, and it shouldn't be relied on.

Changing it to `to_dict` give the impression that you could use that representation for something else, and that it's always going to be a `dict` type. Neither of those things are true, in the future we may decide to introduce a `HAPMessage` type or something similar, which would be returned instead of a `dict`. `to_dict` ties our hands on that, conflating what the function does with what it returns.

On Sun, 30 Oct 2016 at 18:13, DRo <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> The blog post will not come. After having looked at all *live* data feeds
> and although a pattern is more or less there, the next provider would need
> specific development depending on how data and event are delivered.
>
> But the raw summary is:
>
>    - The _load function of the feed is a finite state machine
>
> The following states play a role:
>
>    -
>
>    _ST_OVER: the feed can no longer deliver data. This may be due to a
>    number of circumstances, like having specified the wrong contract or having
>    actually exhausted the data after a historical-only download
>    -
>
>    _ST_FROM: an additional data feed is being used for the initial
>    backfill. The feed will be pushed forward until exhausted and the data
>    copied to self during each iteration
>    -
>
>    _ST_HISTORBACK: used for backfilling and historical only downloads
>    (parameter historical is set to True)
>    -
>
>    _ST_LIVE: The state in which live data is being received. Of course
>    here the detection of problems notified by the *store* plays a role
>
>    There is an additional substate held in _statelivereconn to allow the
>    feed to notify the strategy when the new data (after a reconnection) is
>    LIVE (a buffer of 1 is pending processing)
>
> In _ST_LIVE and _ST_HISTORBACK the received data is passed to auxiliary
> functions that load the data into the lines of the feed.
>
> Connectivity is handled by the corresponding store (in this case
> OandaStore). The store is in charge managing the actual connection model
> and data reception to the provider.
>
> The data feed sits on a queue.Queue object waiting for the *store* to
> push data into it and needs no thread management to listen to anything (for
> example a socket)
>
> It is sensible to think that a generic live feed and live broker could
> define generic interfaces for a, as much as possible, streamlined
> integration from providers.
>
> But connection to providers was added very late into *backtrader* and
> hence the lack of such an interface.

import pandas as pd

def as_table(df):

    for col in df.columns:

Hey, I am afraid the named parameters and tuples are not supported directly. 

Both tuples and lists are converted to Arrays when entering Js2Py and Array. When leaving Js2Py the Array is converted back to list. So tuple will be converted to list during this process. In most cases in Python there is not a big difference between lists and tuples and in most cases they can be used interchangeably. 

Keyword arguments and `*args` are not supported directly yet. 

However, you can solve these problems by creating a wrapper function in **Python**.
    
    def wsgi_application_wrapper(handlers, debug):
        import webapp2
        return webapp2.WSGIApplication(map(tuple, handlers), debug=debug)

Later you add this function to the execution context as usual. 

    ex = js2py.EvalJs({'wsgi_application_wrapper': wsgi_application_wrapper})
    ex.eval("wsgi_application_wrapper([['/', 1],], true)")

Otherwise, you can create a new python file with wrappers and use a pyimport to import it. I understand this is not ideal and maybe I will solve it in the future.

Hello @Avraamu,

I must admit that the `device.read()` method must be documented more thoroughly. What this exception is telling you is that there is nothing to be read from the input device at the moment. Since the device is opened in non-blocking mode, you first have to check if there is anything that can be read from it. One way to do that is with [select](https://docs.python.org/3/library/select.html#select.select). For example:

```python
from select import select

while True:
    # The select() call will block until there are events on dev_obj.
    r, w, x = select([self.dev_obj], [], [])
    # .read() will surely return a list of events now.
    for event in self.dev_obj.read():
        yield event
```

This is exactly what the `device.read_loop()` method does btw. So, your solution might be as simple as modifying your `main()` function to check if the device is readable:

```python
def main():
    controller = ControllerInput(controller_name)
    while True:
        select([controller.dev_obj], [], [])
        controller.get_axis_pos()

# you may remove the 'if event_gen is not None' line from 'get_axis_pos()'
```

One issue with this approach is that your program will only be able to do things when there are events from the pad. This can be addressed in several ways (e.g. `select` has a `timeout` parameter or you could use a thread for input handling). I think issue #15 also touched on this subject.

Let me know if this helps.

Kind regards,
Georgi

First of all, scores are given from the point of view of the side to move.

With multiple threads some instability is to be expected, especially at lower depths. Conversely using a single thread will give completely deterministic results (at least for Stockfish).

If you evaluate a position, and then play the best move and evaluate again, the first score and the flipped second score will be similar most of the time, especially for larger depths. But of course this is not guaranteed. (Otherwise, inductively, the engine would know the evaluation of a "perfect" game of chess.)

Hi Alex,

aside from the aesthetics of your code (clean and concise as always) I found that all but one of my tests passed nicely.

The only miniscule "issue" I found was the rather obscure edge case where a trigger is defined that should not fire as the specified time "02:30 on the last sunday of march each year" always falls into the forward transition of Europe/Berlin.
```
    def test_dst_missing_hour(self, timezone):
        """
        It might be that a CronTrigger can be configured to only ever fire in the missing
        time period during the switch from standard to daylight saving time,
        resulting in a trigger unable to fire.

        """
        trigger = CronTrigger(timezone=timezone, month=3, day='last sun', hour=2, minute=30)
        start_date = timezone.localize(datetime(2006, 4, 1), is_dst=False)
        next_date = trigger.get_next_fire_time(None, start_date)
        assert next_date is None
```
Here, I would expect `next_date` to be `None` but the code gives a timestamp after the end of the unix epoch (2038-01-19T3:14:08 UTC), namely `datetime.datetime(2038, 3, 28, 2, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CET+1:00:00 STD>)`. This issue does not stem from the code in apscheduler (my PR does not cope with it either).
This is very much "good enough" in my opinion. :)
I guess I'd consider including a test like the one above for that obscure edge case (as a documentation of this behavior) in the test suite but with `assert next_date is None or next_date > datetime(2038, 1, 19)` -- "all bets are off after the end of the epoch."

While I value your willingness to contribute, unfortunately I cannot accept this pull request (at least not as a whole). There are two main reasons.

Firstly, there is a purpose for cElementTree being in the tests: the comments mention that once upon a time, it caused a warning message. It is a [regression test](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regression_testing), put there specifically to ensure that some particular bug doesn't turn up again. You can't just ignore cElementTree under Python 3 because it doesn't install, because then we won't know it if the bug returns under Python 3. The only proper way to fix the test for Python 3, is to research why cElementTree was added in the first place and then decide what to do next. We may find either that the regression test is not relevant anymore, in which case we can remove it entirely, or that the test is still relevant, in which case we need to find a replacement for cElementTree which does install under Python 3. See also 9c6a53a. (If this seems overly conservative to you, please read [this](https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2000/04/06/things-you-should-never-do-part-i/)).

Secondly, `pip-review` is a remnant of an old version of the pip-tools package. Many of its current users probably know it from that era, when Python 2.6 was still fully alive. Some of those users might still be stuck with Python 2.6 for other reasons than laziness (see e.g. #2). Right now, `pip-review` still works under Python 2.6 without requiring any additional effort. As long as this is the case, I want to see it from the tests, so those users who are stuck in the past aren't needlessly left in the dark. I also explained this in #15. Concluding, it is too early to remove Python 2.6 from the tests. The problem with Python 2.6 should instead be solved by adding an optional line to the test so that the `pip` warning message about Python 2.6 isn't unexpected anymore.

I do think that some of the changes you propose would be useful. I would be grateful if you could submit those changes in a separate, smaller pull request. Please see my comments in the "Files changed" tab for some details.

There is an issue where the list may be empty. Suppose you have:

~~~
        <servers>
        </servers>
~~~

then this parses as:

~~~
{
    "servers": None
}
~~~

Then the user code needs to look something like this:

~~~
        if data['servers']:
            servers = data['servers']['server']
        else:
            servers = []
        for server in servers:
            print server['name'], server['os']
~~~

Or more obtusely:

~~~
        for server in (data['servers'] or {'server':[]})['server']:
            print server['name'], server['os']
~~~

To fix this would require indicating the expected child for each parent. What do people think about `force_list={'servers': 'server'}` ? This would mean:

1. A `<servers>` tag would always generate a `"server": [...]` child in the parsed output, with zero or more elements
2. Conversely, a random `<server>` tag would *not* be forced to a list unless it is directly inside a `<servers>` tag

Actually, in the script, I created 2 properties named "overridden" and "simulated". 
If you simulate an input (setting the out_of_service flag to True and the presentValue to whatever you want) the "simulated" flag will turn on. Same with the overridden if you ever write to priority 8.

But those are set by the app so if you connect to a network, you can't actually tell quickly if some points are set to a certain priority.

To release, we have to send a "null" value to the priority.
BAC0 make this easy in the context of an override or a simulation.

Example with an output : 
```
dev['analogOutput_for_a_valve'] = 100
# This will "override" the output... because it's an output and typically, if you want to write to an output, you want to make an override...
dev['analogOutput_for_a_valve'] = 'auto'
# This will release the override
```
Example with a simulation
```
dev['an_analogInput_temperature_sensor'] = 23
# This will "simulate" the value because if you want to write to a physical analog Input, you probably want to simulate something else...
dev['an_analogInput_temperature_sensor'] = 'auto'
# Revert the simulation
```

I don't have access to many IP devices... the ones I've been able to connect to were quite easily accessed. You need to know the IP address and the device ID.
Code would look like : 
```
import BAC0
bacnet = BAC0.connect()

controller = BAC0.device('ip', deviceId, bacnet)
```

As a starting point, to verify if your computer sees some devices, you can use (once `bacnet`is defined) : 
```
bacnet.whois()
bacnet.devices
```
it'll list every devices on your network

Removal of `typing` from the standard library is unlikely but we'll see. But the import time is an issue on its own.

As for "dataclasses should be unaware of typing", that sounds reasonable if ClassVar is meant to be used by data classes users anyway.

1. It's rather simple for typeshed to include the alias from dataclasses.ClassVar to typing.ClassVar so they would be equivalent for typing purposes.
2. And with the singledispatch approach, typing.ClassVar would register to respond to `dataclasses.is_class_var()`. I don't like that `is_class_var()` makes `dataclasses.ClassVar` a ghetto ABC that requires use of a custom function instead of regular `isinstance()` and `issubclass()`.

Also note that while this makes importing `dataclasses` faster, it further slows down importing `typing` which now will have to attempt to import `dataclasses` and set up the singledispatch.

Wow. Lots of fans of emojis here. :-)

Hynek's three bullets [above](https://github.com/ericvsmith/dataclasses/issues/19#issuecomment-310895760) make a rather strong case against it. The third bullet ("a versioned and upgradeable instance of attrs part of the stdlib") leaves the way open but doesn't answer how -- the pip way is certainly not reasonable (what pip does is bundle a 3rd party package which can then be upgraded independent from the stdlib, which is fine for pip but not for something that the rest of the stdlib may depend on it). We've also had the xml stdlib module which explicitly supported installing extensions as a 3rd party package, and that turned out terrible. (People wrote code depending on the 3rd party package but didn't know it.)

But maybe I should also explain why *I* am against just incorporating attrs in the stdlib. I don't like the color it paints the bikeshed -- not the package name itself, not the "cute" names, not the basic way of declaring that something is an attribute (it should be an annotation, not an initialization). I like many of attrs' other ideas though, so I'd like to build a bikeshed for the stdlib in my own colors. If eventually Hynek likes the color scheme used by the stdlib he's welcome to repaint his shed.

Having a standard API will also make it simpler to add static type checking (PEP 484 style) for classes using dataclasses (or whatever name we pick, as long as it's not attrs :-). E.g. mypy should learn to infer the signature of the constructor added by the class decorator.

In general new stdlib modules should be designed with stdlib inclusion first and foremost in mind (like we did with asyncio). Almost every time a successful existing 3rd party module is proposed as a stdlib candidate we find it's not going to work.

I disagree with Hynek that calling these "data classes" sends the wrong message. The things the proposed decorator adds to a class all have to do with the "data" aspect of the class: how to initialize the instance variables, how to print (repr) them, how to compare/hash them. None of this precludes that the class may also have methods. (In fact the ultimate read-only data class, NamedTuple, allows adding methods in Python 3.6.

To the contrary, I think "auto" is a terrible name, because it doesn't specify *what* is automatic here.

@julienduchesne Apologies for letting this sit so long. My personal life has been really busy lately, and I haven't had much time to dedicate to working on projects.

You're right, ``_update_limits_from_api`` is probably the more logical place. I'm sorry I didn't think of that myself.

I think I'm still a bit torn on the implementation of this. Right now, the actual set of limits that awslimitchecker returns in its output is a fixed, known set. With the current implementation, the number of limits we return will be increased by the number of hosted zones in an account. This also makes it significantly more difficult to suppress some checks (i.e. all hosted zone checks) or to programmatically work with check results across multiple accounts. This is a problem that awslimitchecker hasn't had to deal with before, because this is the first limit we monitor that doesn't have a fixed set of limits (i.e. even for the instance type limits, we know in the source code what instance types there are and what the default limits for them are).

I'm sort of thinking of perhaps implementing a subclass of AwsLimit (or AwsLimitUsage) for this, which would allow having _one_ hosted zone VPCs limit and _one_ rrsets limit, and then storing usage much like we do for things like subnets per vpc. i.e. I'm thinking of something that would store usage/threshold pairs for each hosted zone. But that would require some changes to the public API of how limits and usage work.

Alternatively we could just subclass AwsLimit without any actual changes, i.e. just an empty wrapper class, called something like ``HostedZoneLimit``... and then tell people who are using the Python API that if they want to handle these limits specially, they can do so based on that class.

The most common reason for such problems is mixing `sudo` and non-`sudo` commands (e.g. many people would automatically write `sudo apt install` but `pip install`).

In general, I personally prefer to first set up a local `virtualenv`, and then install everything into this virtualenv using a non-sudo `pip`, this is usually the most reliable approach.

Mixing `apt`, `pip` and `conda` packages is a sure recipe for confusion.

The issue is if there's malformed input. If someone writes

FYI @omab @cclay

For the provider I'm implementing I now made it so that every time it gets an access token, the `expires_in` (from the response) is stored in seconds, and right away `expires` is calculated as timestamp and also stored in `extra_data`. I could extend this and provide a pull request so that the base class always stores it that way for all providers, unless the values are somehow already set.

As far as I can see, that would not affect current workings, because the library already checks if the value in `expires` is a timestamp or a number of seconds. It would furthermore bring it closer to the OAuth2 standard, where `expires_in` is defined (as seconds) whereas this library only relies on `expires` (with mixed content).

Yes, it does make it simpler. As KNN tries to find the k nearest neighbors it doesn't mean that it goes through all the data points to find those k nearest points. We can safely assume that the model has already constructed a graph denoting all training data points and now the model needs to search the region in which the new unseen point falls. This might appear to be a silly reasoning but it does matter because finding k nearest points really doesn't take O(n) time.

@oknoproblem3 You don't really have to recompile PostgreSQL to workaround CUBE limitation.
Having your 128 points split into two vectors (64 + 64 for example) you can calculate euclidean distance of whole vector from euclidean distances of two sub vectors:
```python
            query = "SELECT id, sqrt(power(CUBE(array[{}]) <-> vec_low, 2) + power(CUBE(array[{}]) <-> vec_high, 2)) as dist FROM encodings ORDER BY dist ASC LIMIT 1".format(
                ','.join(str(s) for s in encodings[0][0:63]),
                ','.join(str(s) for s in encodings[0][64:127]),
            )
```
`dist` in this expression will be valid to check against the threshold of `0.6`.

> Agree, and one solution might be to remove the predict method altogether. The consequences of this are we then require k_fold_cross_validation to accept a mandatory prediction method, and we are offloading the problem on to packages like https://github.com/sashaostr/sklearn-lifelines.

I have to say I'm a fan of this, or something like it. [Explicit is better than implicit](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/) anyway :)

To me, `predict()` intuitively means something like "give me the prediction that most people want from this particular model most of the time" (at least if you're borrowing its meaning in R). But that requires the output type of `predict` to be almost completely unconstrained, e.g. the Aalen model's natural prediction is multidimensional and the Cox model's natural prediction isn't even probabilities, only relative rankings.

Hi aborruso,

You are trying to use cell magic (%%sql) which will treat the whole cell as a multi-line sql statement.  I believe you want to use line magic (%sql) to embed the query inside of code within a cell - the challenge is that you need to have the sql statement on a single line.  To get around this (and use mutliple lines) you may use Python's line continuation by ending a line with the backslash character.

I believe your code could look something like this:

```python
list=[1,2]
for i in list:
    %sql update "table" \
    set fieldA = 12 \
    where fieldB=:i;
```

Hope this helps.

`Child1.objects.order_by('date')` will always perform an INNER JOIN on the `base` table anyway so the filter by content type is redundant here.

If you want to perform a `SELECT * FROM base WHERE content_type_id = ? ORDER BY date` you can use the `select_subclasses` API:

```python
Base.objects.select_subclasses(Child1).order_by('date')
```

I think the middleware in the django cms automatically appends the language to the url, it should not be needed to add it to the URL, as far as i know. In that case, the current request should always know the language being used, and you could query models like TranslatableModel.objects.language().all() - then the slug you get back would be in the same language as the request.

If you want to get the value of the slug from another language, on a method on the model, you will need to make another query. This could be done by querying the translations tabel, something like:


```python
self._meta.translations_model.get(language=language, master__pk=self.pk).slug
```

Kind regards,

Kristian

The link wasn't any more correct before, since `*` in standard UNIX glob patterns doesn't match across directory boundaries. Sure, `**` is a non-standard extension too (but not specific to the `pathmatch` module).


So now what? We could either accept that `**` is an undocumented feature (matter to change), just like the old (undocumented) semantics of `*`, or we could standardize something (i.e. put it in the README and test suite).

As far as I'm concerned, I don't have any need for recursive glob patterns, as my own use case is merely to deal with legacy code (so if more than one file requires the same ingnores it's mere coincidence).

But I'm aware that I'm no longer the only user of this, and want to keep this a useful tool for others. So I'd like to see what @The-Compiler, @kvas-it and @jonafato (and others who use this in production) think?

I've been using a version that was proposed in a Django ticket for years now, that code essentially comes from this patch: https://code.djangoproject.com/attachment/ticket/14512/ticket14512.diff

Usually this decorator wraps other decorator invocations that are meant for decorating an FBV. I've simply unwrapped the wrapped decorator and that's where the name 'squashed' comes from. 'Unwrapped' may have been a better choice, but I didn't want to use that term because it has a slightly different (albeit similar) meaning in `decorators.py`.

I've found a shorter version, here, which isn't EXACTLY the same thing but seems to be for my purposes:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/6069070/how-to-use-permission-required-decorators-on-django-class-based-views

The tricky thing here is, the shorter version does not seem to exercise the bug because of how it's constructed. Since the scoping is a bit different when `method_decorator` is used for the inner decoration, it doesn't seem to mess with the freevars. In any event, I still feel like relying on the coincidence of argument position is more fragile and less correct than relying on the fact that the view callable must necessarily be an instance that is `FunctionType`. While `FunctionType` type is still a  coincidental detail, it has more of a chance to be right. The first argument will then need to also be a FunctionType, and so any time the previous predicate was a true positive it will also be so in this case. Additionally where it was a false positive, it will no longer be one, and where it was a false negative at least there's now an opportunity to find the view callable in other positions.

OK, so we are sending droplet IDs as numbers, whereas the DigitalOcean API expects strings. Here are the reference pages: https://developers.digitalocean.com/documentation/v2/#tag-a-resource

I think there are a number of "right" ways to do this. I just made a change that supports two of those ways though:

1. The way that you have laid out above will now work as of version 1.4.1
2. You can also use the `renderer_context`, which would look something like:

```python
class MyViewSet(..):
  renderer_classes = renderer_classes = api_settings.DEFAULT_RENDERER_CLASSES + [r.CSVRenderer, ]

  def get_renderer_context(self):
    context = super().get_renderer_context()
    context['writer_opts'] = {
      'quoting': csv.QUOTE_ALL,
      'quotechar': '"',
      'delimiter': ';'
      }
    return context
```

If you want to change the behavior project-wide, I suppose you could just override the value once at the top of your *views.py*, i.e.:

```python
r.CSVRenderer.writer_opts = {
  'quoting': csv.QUOTE_ALL,
  'quotechar': '"',
  'delimiter': ';'
  }
```

Though, in that case you're right that using the Django settings might be more appropriate (but is also not implemented right now).

Hi, @farhatnawaz!

You actually provided the document, but not serializer.

I have a feeling that this is related to the fact that in DRF3.3 `fields` argument for serializer became mandatory, so now your `UserSerializer` will look something like this:

```
class UserSerializer(DocumentSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = User
        fields = ('__all__')
```

This particular example from TF demonstrates their "tower" architecture for multi-GPU training.  I converted this example as-is, without trying to distribute it (as you are now trying).  That said, it looks like [someone else has tried to convert it to distributed TensorFlow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42707868/tensorflow-cifar10-distributed-training-accuracy), but was getting poor accuracy, and [someone else encountered device placement errors](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35909427/tensorflow-distributed-runtime-model-parallel-cifar-10) when trying to distribute it.

So if you haven't already done so, I'd probably try to get the latest CIFAR-10 example and try to distribute it, but even then, it seems unclear if distribution would help or hurt in this particular architecture/example.

Note: in your example above, you should probably set `num_ps = 1` for distributed TF, and remove the lines that delete the train directory if it already exists (since you may have one note writing it, and another node later deleting it).  Also, in your code, it looks like you are only running the training session  in the `if job_name == "worker" and task_index == 0:` conditional block.  This would basically skip all this code on the other workers.

@abshkd, the code does look simple enough and should work given the right conditions which I am detailing below.  But can you clarify your comment here: `m_doc = db.get(q.get()) #q is a list of _id`?  Is *q* really a python `list`?  If so, how do you perform a `get()` call on it?  Also, are you getting any errors?  If so can you send them as well?

I am going to assume that `q.get()` returns a valid _id for a document and that you are NOT getting any error when running this code.  Based on these two assumptions, the only way for your code snippet to work is if the db object already has the Document stored in its locally cached dictionary.  This can happen either if you had previously executed the `db.create_document(...)` method call for that document or you had retrieved the document from the remote database and into the db locally cached dictionary with `db['...']`. The example below illustrates this.  It includes your code snippet in its entirety.  I have validated that the example does in fact work.  Can you take a look at it and see if any of this helps you along?  If not, please send the full code block from "client connect" through "disconnect" and I think we can definitely resolve the problem.

Also fwiw, `db.get('docid')` will only get the locally cached Document if it is there while `db['docid']` will attempt to get the document from the remote database if it cannot find it in the local "db" dictionary.

```python
class Q(object):
    def __init__(self, id):
        self._my_id = id
        
    def get(self):
        return self._my_id

q = Q('my-generated-id-001')

def my_func(x):
    return 123

from cloudant import Cloudant

client = Cloudant(USERNAME, PASSWORD, account=ACCOUNT)
client.connect()
db = client['my_database']
# displays the locally cached "db" dictionary - should be empty
print 'local cache before doc create: {0}'.format(db) 
# Creates the document remotely and stores it locally in the "db" dictionary
m_doc = db.create_document({'_id': q.get()})
print 'm_doc after create: {0}'.format(m_doc)
# displays the locally cached "db" dictionary - should contain m_doc document
print 'local cache after doc create: {0}'.format(db)
del db[q.get()] # Clear the locally cached "db" dictionary
# displays the locally cached "db" dictionary - should be empty now
print 'local cache before doc retrieval (after clearing cache): {0}'.format(db)
# Retrieve the document and store it in the locally cached "db" dictionary
db[q.get()] 
# displays the locally cached "db" dictionary - should contain m_doc document
print 'local cache after doc retrieval: {0}'.format(db) 

del m_doc
# Your code block
m_doc = db.get(q.get()) #q is a list of _id
m_doc['start'] = my_func('block_address')#my_func returns an int
m_doc.save()

print 'm_doc after update: {0}'.format(m_doc)
client.disconnect()
```

The memory used by MKLDNN is also allocated by the MXNet memory allocator. MKLDNN NDArrays are reused in the computation graph as normal NDArrays. The difference between MKLDNN operators and the normal operators is the temp space. MKLDNN operators use it for layout conversion. The temp space isn't required by most of the normal operators. The temp space may consume huge amount of memory and the MXNet executor may maintain multiple temp space in order to execute multiple operators simultaneously.

@pengzhao-intel could you investigate the issue further? I think we should reduce the unnecessary memory consumption.

The effect that prompted this PR is that LazyConfigValues used in config files:

```python
c.NotebookApp.server_extensions.append('myextension')
```

end up being added ~three times, due to how we load config files. LazyConfigValues memoize their evaluated value, but when we are loading config, we are always copying the config object in the merge, so the memo is never re-used. This ends up with

```python
server_extensions = ['myextension', 'myextension', 'myextension']
```

I don't think this is a huge deal because:

0. in dict/set traits, it doesn't have any effect, only lists
0. In the majority of configurable list traits I'm aware of, adding an item multiple times doesn't actually have a negative effect, just a minor inefficiency
0. It's entirely possible that nobody but me actually uses this feature

I noticed this when I was working on the ipyparallel server extension, which I saw was getting loaded multiple times when I started adding extra debug logging.

After reading through [that Xbox paper](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/XboxInnerProduct.pdf) and trying a bunch of tests on random and real-world data, I've updated this PR with a solution that's a bit more involved, but is now much more efficient than just straight dot product.

The new method adds:
 - an optional number of `internal_dimensions` added to the index by Annoy when using the `dot` distance measure (it adds exactly one internal dimension, but this is configurable in case other metrics need to use this in the future)
 - a `preprocess` step, called from `build`, that populates this extra internal dimension before any queries are made
 - logic to all of the `get_nns_*` methods to pad out input vectors with zeros in the internal dimensions, as per the paper

My tests show that on random gaussian-distributed data, this method seems to actually outperform regular cosine distance for accuracy when fetching 10 neighbours (using kendall-tau rank correlation as a metric):

```
Average kendall-tau accuracy:
at search_k=10: 	cosine 		7.01%
at search_k=10: 	Xbox trick 	12.28%
at search_k=10: 	Annoy Dot 	12.28%

at search_k=50: 	Xbox trick 	18.72%
at search_k=50: 	Annoy Dot 	18.72%

at search_k=100: 	cosine 		13.63%
at search_k=100: 	Xbox trick 	24.68%
at search_k=100: 	Annoy Dot 	24.68%

at search_k=200: 	cosine 		23.74%
at search_k=200: 	Xbox trick 	35.03%
at search_k=200: 	Annoy Dot 	35.03%

at search_k=500: 	cosine 		54.44%
at search_k=500: 	Xbox trick 	60.78%
at search_k=500: 	Annoy Dot 	60.78%

at search_k=1000: 	cosine 		85.14%

at search_k=1000: 	Annoy Dot 	81.28%

In this case, when you create `plain_msg` that is where the LiteralData packet is created - you got it in binary form (along with any signatures if you had also signed it) when you called `plain_msg.__bytes__()`.

> Specifies the integration's Uniform Resource Identifier (URI). For HTTP integrations, the URI must be a fully formed, encoded HTTP(S) URL according to the [RFC-3986 specification](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc3986.txt). For AWS integrations, the URI should be of the form `arn:aws:apigateway:{region}:{subdomain.service|service}:{path|action}/{service_api}`. `Region`, `subdomain` and `service` are used to determine the right endpoint. For AWS services that use the `Action=` query string parameter, `service_api` should be a valid action for the desired service. For RESTful AWS service APIs, `path` is used to indicate that the remaining substring in the URI should be treated as the path to the resource, including the initial `/`.

There was also some discussion on this point in the [javascript repo](https://github.com/aws/aws-sdk-js/issues/868).

No, a middleware component outside of pyramid would do the retry. This is how repoze.tm2 worked (the code that pyramid_tm replaced). For end users, if you’re new to python and you see that you can have 5 sections you start thinking maybe you should. Then every time you try to use pipenv you can’t remember what section you put a package in. You don’t use `cat Pipfile` because you’re new, you have to open it in an editor and this is a slow process. You get frustrated.                "{0!r} does not exist in evaluation environment.".format(name)

However, `invalid_marker` is just trying to check if a marker is valid or not, so I wondered about catching the exception there and just saying it's not valid (by returning the exception rather than `False`).  So I created this patch.

```
diff --git a/pkg_resources/__init__.py b/pkg_resources/__init__.py
index 7333464..71f614e 100644
--- a/pkg_resources/__init__.py
+++ b/pkg_resources/__init__.py
@@ -1439,6 +1439,8 @@ def invalid_marker(text):
     """
     try:
         evaluate_marker(text)
+    except packaging.markers.UndefinedEnvironmentName as e:
+        return e
     except SyntaxError as e:
         e.filename = None
         e.lineno = None
diff --git a/setuptools/tests/test_egg_info.py b/setuptools/tests/test_egg_info.py
index 66ca916..b2cd384 100644
--- a/setuptools/tests/test_egg_info.py
+++ b/setuptools/tests/test_egg_info.py
@@ -284,6 +284,19 @@ class TestEggInfo(object):
         ''',
 
         '''
+        install_requires_with_extra_marker
+
+        install_requires=["barbazquux; extra != 'foo'"],
+
+        [options]
+        install_requires =
+            barbazquux; extra != 'foo'
+
+        [:extra != "foo"]
+        barbazquux
+        ''',
+
+        '''
```

For Jython; this is necessary because it was attempting to write str data to text files. I don't have enough insight this moment to know if this is what really should be done or not, but seems mostly harmless:
```
diff -r 67210f355576 Lib/_jyio.py
--- a/Lib/_jyio.py	Mon Mar 02 22:24:31 2015 -0700
+++ b/Lib/_jyio.py	Wed Mar 04 11:53:35 2015 -0700
@@ -1152,8 +1152,11 @@
         if self.closed:
             raise ValueError("write to closed file")
         if not isinstance(s, unicode):
-            raise TypeError("can't write %s to text stream" %
-                            s.__class__.__name__)
+            try:
+                s.decode("ascii")
+            except UnicodeDecodeError:
+                raise TypeError("can't write %s: %s to text stream" %
+                                (s, s.__class__.__name__))
         length = len(s)
         haslf = (self._writetranslate or self._line_buffering) and "\n" in s
         if haslf and self._writetranslate and self._writenl != "\n":
```

Then avoid using wheels, because they need to be from our bundle:
```
~/jythondev/jython27/dist/bin/virtualenv --no-pip --no-setuptools baz
```

But the resulting baz/bin directory is a bit strange:
```
$ ls baz/bin
activate.csh		activate.sh		python			python2.7
activate.fish		activate_this.py	python2
```

Naturally python, python2, python2.7 actually are copies of the bin/jython startup script. (I would expect symbolic links, but perhaps this is defaulting to copying if it doesn't realize it's on posix, our infamous os.name vs os._name distinction in Jython.)

Unfortunately this bash script then fails because it cannot find the actual jar. But setting `$JYTHON_HOME` fixes that, albeit at the cost of one dependency that virtualenv should likely take care of.

Aha, thanks - so if that's the use case, I'd actually suggest that you keep
count of the number of messages you produced, and then dequeue just as many
delivery requests. While that may not sound particularly elegant, it would
in fact be safer than matching the last message produced, because if you
have a nonzero `max_retries` your delivery reports could be out of order.
On the other hand, there's a hard guarantee that there shall be exactly 1
delivery report for each `produce()` (provided that did not raise an
exception directly). I should add that to the delivery-reporting docs.

We've been working with the convention that underscore-prefixed attributes
are considered private, and you're definitely right that that also deserves
a clear mention somewhere in the docs.

I'd like to chime in. I've been looking at implementing multichannel histograms (PR #2788), and there are some problems that result from not having an explicit range parameter available:

- With just nbins and source_range=image,  there is no way to produce the same binning for different channels, since the actual bins will depend on the data in each channel.

- Similarly, the offset calculation for multichannel images would need to be done for the full array and not for each channel separately to match bins.

I'm also a bit concerned with the default to dtype and existing limits for float images. I encounter float images that are way off the (0,1) and (-1,1) ranges daily, and silently defaulting to a range smaller than image min/max is not desirable for a histogram for those cases. While I see why most scikit-image algorithms want and expect floats to be in a certain range, for histograms it doesn't seem warranted.

For context, I do hyperspectral imaging research and our group is looking at using scikit-image for some of the workflow, and I got looking at the code during the Euroscipy 2017 sprints.

I just noticed that the fix of negative values introduced in commit 8042dc2c in lab2xyz and xyz2rgb actually blew my small colormap creator script https://github.com/pierre-haessig/lab-colormap-creator

As you'll see on this screenshot, I was relying on getting negative values to *detect which Lab values *that are out of the sRGB gamut, and then apply my own saturation function, which is a kind of "artistic graying".
![editor_screenshot](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1010404/12351463/fd60171a-bb7d-11e5-8d79-39be6c7c96c9.png)

```python
def rgb_saturate(rgb, gray_out=0.2):
    '''inplace saturation of values outside [0,1].
    `rgb` should be a (..., 3) shaped array
    `gray_out` should be between 0 and 1
    
    '''
    rgb = np.atleast_2d(rgb)
    assert 0 <= gray_out <= 1
    # Detect pixel saturation, in *any* of the R,G,B channel
    sat = ((rgb<0) | (rgb>1)).sum(axis=-1).astype(bool)

    # Apply channel saturation
    rgb[rgb>1] = 1
    rgb[rgb<0] = 0

    # Gray out pixels in the saturated zones
    rgb[sat] = 0.5 + (rgb[sat]-0.5)*gray_out
    
    return rgb, sat
```

@scottpurdy: regarding
> High level looks good but I need to review more thoroughly. One question first: is adding the L special field necessary for this change or is it just another thing that you think should be added?

I noticed that `RecordStream` and `FileRecordStream` are hard-coding the attributes instead of making use of making use of the constants in `FieldMetaSpecial`. Support for `L` (learning attribute) had been hard-coded in those classes without updating updating `FieldMetaSpecial`. Circumventing the intended classes constants is a bad situation, so I set good precedent by updating `FieldMetaSpecial` to include the new attribute and modifying the code to use `FieldMetaSpecial` constants instead of hard-coded values. The new `RecordDictEncoder` relies on `FieldMetaSpecial`, so I also felt that it was in-scope to make that change.

**NOTE** that a similar problem exists with the use of hard-coded types instead of `FieldMetaType`-based constants in `FileRecordStream` and elsewhere. That one had diverged even more than `FieldMetaSpecial`, including new types and support for both upper-case and lower-case variants. So, I ended up leaving the field type code alone, as it was more involved to rein in and completely out of scope of the change that I needed to make.

>  I have some greenlets writing changes to a BTree, and another greenlet committing the changes... I probably should have used a separate transaction manager for every single greenlet. 

I suspect that's your problem. BTrees are not safe for concurrent use; they do *no* locking to protect themselves from having inconsistent internal states in that case. They're meant to be used within a single thread at a time only.  In general, I think ZODB Connection and Persistent objects are not meant for concurrent use---best case scenario is that they load their state out of the database multiple times; worst case scenario their state gets corrupted and you get errors like this. Asynchronously commiting that transaction and thus syncing the connection doesn't help matters. (Note that I mean identical objects, where `id(x) == id(y)`; each thread can load the same key from its *own* Connection and access it that way, but then each thread has its own copy of the object.)

For this meaning, threads and greenlets are equivalent. They both potentially provide concurrency where concurrency was not expected. 

- Using ZEO with greenlets allows lots of concurrency as each ZEO operation crosses a socket, allowing a greenlet switch to occur.
- Using greenlets with a FileStorage, in contrast, allows for no automatic concurrency because file operations do not switch greenlets, effectively serializing database access.
- Using native threads with a FileStorage, however, might expose the same issues.

OK, I was able to reproduce this error with gcc 6.2.

It looks like boost 1.63 is broken with respect to the way it does the c++11 smart pointers.  (Or specifically respecting our #defines that tell them *not* to use c++11 pointers.)  

The workaround is to compile with `EXTRA_FLAGS=-std=c++98`.  Or as you found, to downgrade to boost 1.60.  Or using gcc 5 (or 4) should also work.

AFAICT, it's just the combination of gcc 6 with its default std=c++14 and boost 1.63 that is broken.

Of all the replies here, only one really is the answer.  The problem is when you're trying to set a space using CSS and not the FA classes.  You can't do a content: "\f101 " or content: " \f101" or whatever.  THAT'S why people are asking for a space character, so they can specify one via custom CSS.  Why would we do that?  Because sometimes you need to change the DOM like on a mouse-over or when something changes.

In any case, the solution is to use content: "\f101\A0" or content: "\A0\f101" or whatever you want.  The "\A0" is the "trick" to add a space to FA when using a content CSS attribute.

I think the configuration with a primary and fallback methods make sense, but I'd change it to include just the name of the method, i.e repica_method = 'wal-e' and specify the actual script in the [wal-e] section. The rationale is that there are likely several configuration parameters required to run the script, and it would be easier to configure them all and the script full path in one section. We might also serialize the content of the configuration section to the script being called.  Obviously, basebackup would be an exception from this rule...

Actually, providing a list to the  replica_method would spare us one parameter, i.e:
replica_methods = 'basebackup, wal-e'.
Patroni might not assume anything if this parameter is empty and just terminate with an error.

By default, this will be set to 
replica_methods = 'basebackup'.

As for the retry, I think patroni should do retries the same way for any replica method, i.e. it should try to create the new replica N times, after which it would log the failure and terminate.

@davecramer we can't, because the individual node simply doesn't know.  It knows that it's waiting for the master to become available so it can bootstrap, which is what it will tell you.

For posterity, let me explain two hypothetical situations, which are identical from the individual node's point of view:

## Situation 1:  You intentionally delete all pods in order to delete the cluster.

In this situation, there's still an initialization key (on Kubernetes, in the form of a configmap) for the cluster by that name.  This key doesn't get deleted when you do a `kubectl delete -f patroni.yaml` because it was created by the master in the original cluster, and thus isn't part of the yaml file.  Operator implementations do delete the key, as a separate action.

This means that when the postgres node comes up, it knows that it doesn't have any data.  It checks to see if the cluster has already been initialized, which means it *should have* data, and finds that yes, it has been.  So now it knows that it's not the master (because no data) so it's going to wait for a master to show up, however long that takes.

This is the common "cluster will not start" situation.  It's regrettable and provides a bad out-of-box experience but is also unavoidable because of Case 2.

## 2: Combination Failure

1. You have a 3-pod postgres cluster, postgres-0,-1,-2.
2. postgres-0 is currently the master.
3. Your kubernetes cluster goes down, taking all nodes offline.
4. Your kubernetes cluster comes back up, but the physical node housing postgres-0 does not, and the nodes housing postgres-1 and postgres-2 are slow to rejoin the cluster.
5. As a result, kubernetes schedules a replacement for postgres-0 *before* postgres-1 or postgres-2 have come back up.
6. postgres-0 sees that the master key has expired.
7. postgres-0 sees the initialization key, so it knows that there must be data *somewhere*, so it waits for a pod with data to show up.
8. postgres-1 reconnects to the cluster, obtains the master key, and promotes itself to master.
9. postgres-0 starts replication from postgres-1.

The critical part of this sequence is what happens at step 7. From the perspective of postgres-0, its situation is absolutely identical to Case 1.  There's an initialization key, the master key is invalid, and it has no data.  At this point, there's no way for postgres-0 to know, or to write to its log, which of the two cases you are in because it simply doesn't know.

This is why my proposal for making this situation better is to wait for 6 polling cycles, or so, and then emit a more generic error message, like:

"Still waiting for master to bootstrap.   Did you recreate a cluster with the same name as a deleted cluster?  If so, you may need to remove the initialization key at {configmap-location}.  WARNING: removing the initialization key on a live cluster will erase all data."

@davissp14, Patroni needs to know only one host from etcd cluster.

Every etcd node can report etcd cluster topology via  `/v2/machines` REST API call, and Patroni is using it: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/dcs/etcd.py#L124

If one of the requests to etcd failed, Patroni switches to another etcd node, re-executing request and refreshes topology of etcd cluster.
In addition to that Patroni also doing it (refreshes etcd cluster topology) periodically, every 5 minutes.
If you move one of etcd node to another host and another ip, Patroni will discover it maximum in 5 minutes.

Why did we choose a 5 minutes? Because it nearly impossible to move all etcd nodes to a different ips/hosts.

And at the end we can return to your original question.
Yes, we can make it possible to specify multiple etcd hosts in the configuration, but how it should behave later, if for some reason all etcd nodes will become not accessible?

@ahmedsajid  Yes, I had to solve that too. I have extended "JIRA" so `self` is the jira object. It will also require a `_gain_sudo_session(...)` before calling it, I've deliberately duplicated the behaviour of existing functions that operate on admin-interface forms in this.

The below code is mostly (save the error logging part) ready to include into the project as well.

```python
def activate_user(self, username):
    """Enable/activate the user."""
    url = self._options['server'] + '/secure/admin/user/EditUser.jspa'
    self._options['headers']['Content-Type'] = 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=UTF-8'
    user = self.user(username)
    userInfo = {
        'inline': 'true',
        'decorator': 'dialog',
        'username': user.name,
        'fullName': user.displayName,
        'email': user.emailAddress,
        'editName': user.name,
        'active': 'true'
    }

    try:
        r = self._session.post(url, headers=self._options['headers'], data=userInfo)
        if r.status_code == 200:
            return True
        else:
            logging.warning(
                'Got response from activating %s: %s' % (username, r.status_code))
            return r.status_code
    except Exception as e:
        print("Error Activating %s: %s" % (username, e))

```

Kevin, first up I'll be the first to say it's very possible I'm doing something stupid here, so please take what I say here with a grain of salt and if you have suggestions on how I can do it better I'm all ears.

Originally I started building my own login form but then I realized I actually needed registration, verification, login, MFA, forgot password, and forgot username workflows.  Allowing Cognito to manage all that takes some burden off us white we're building out the MVP.  To me that stuff is not a huge value add right now - I want my team focused on building the app and user management stuff is not key to that.  Not having to deal with storing people's passwords is pretty nice as well - the less of those kinds of problems I have to solve the better.  

Doing it this way also keeps things open for using more than just Cognito.  If I want to integrate with Azure AD, Auth0, or something else it would be pretty easy to add them now.  I don't see us doing that soon, but it's doable.

If we were replacing the authentication system for an existing app and I needed a lot of control over those experiences then clearly I wouldn't use their hosted pages.

There are two ways to do sharding. Splitting lexicographical or by using the scatter property. The later is better, and used if it is possible automatically. If you are doing a MR over all entities of a given type but that have strangely distributed IDs, that should just work out of the box, as it will use the scatter property to find the split points. So I'm assuming your problem is actually more complicated and that you mean to say that your table looks like:
123-a
123-b
...
456-abc-1
...
456-abc-200
...
456-def-1
...
789-a

and you only want to include the ones starting with "456" but the sub-ranges "abc" and "def" under it are very uneven. In this case you need to cajole your use-case into working with scatter. Using a prefix match will not help with this because a prefix of 456 is going to get split into "id >= 456 and id < 457" which means the only way to split that up is lexicographical. Instead you can add a property that is "456" to the entities. Then if you do a MR over the keyspace with a filter on the property "newproperty=456" then you can use it as a filter and it will split accurately regardless of how things are distributed by ID.

> When I say root package, I mean a python package that represents the root namespace "google" so that only one package is writing to that init file. It seems that the cloud core would write an init to "google/cloud/" for example. So google core would write to "google/".

That's not quite how namespace packages work. `google-cloud-core` puts code in `google.cloud`, so there is nothing to place in the `google` namespace package (other than the `google.cloud` subpackage).

As @duggelz notes, your best bet is to probably package up all of the files in a self-contained zip. You'll be better off doing this by installing into a virtualenv and then zipping what's installed. (Due to the lack of an obvious way to get Spark to support namespace packages, you will still need to `touch` the `__init__.py` files as a workaround).

```
$ virtualenv venv
$ venv/bin/pip install google-cloud-storage
$ touch venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/__init__.py
$ touch venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/__init__.py
$ zip -r all_of_it.zip venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/
```

(i.e. I'm suggesting you use something other than the wheel for your zip).
