Hello,
One of the challenges of maintaining a large open source project is that sometimes, you can bite off more than you can chew. As the lead maintainer of `google-cloud-python`, I can definitely say that I have let the issues here pile up.

I am :-1: to this approach. For now, we support both JSON-over-HTTP (or protobuf-over-HTTP in the case of `datastore`) and protobuf-over-gRPC APIs. As a result, the same calls, e.g. `Client.put()` can result in very different (backend exceptions). Our goal is to raise the same exception for either backend.

So with that goal in mind, I imagined a fix where we just allow more optional metadata to be passed during the construction of our current exceptions. For example [`UNKNOWN`][1], [`INTERNAL`][2] and [`DATA_LOSS`][3] all map to 500 (`InternalServerError`), which loses information. But if we code get more info, the data could be preserved, e.g.

```python
>>> try:
...     do_something()
... except InternalServerError as exc:
...     print(exc.grpc_code)
...
<StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE: (14, 'unavailable')>
```

[1]: https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/f5003093e203f24dcd72769cd638b20110a433f1/google/rpc/code.proto#L50
[2]: https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/f5003093e203f24dcd72769cd638b20110a433f1/google/rpc/code.proto#L163
[3]: https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/f5003093e203f24dcd72769cd638b20110a433f1/google/rpc/code.proto#L178

Hi, thanks for the contribution. Most Linux systems do not allow usernames that are longer than 32 characters and OS Login does not permit users to set their username beyond that length. When you attempt to SSH into an instance, you should be using the username in your login profile (the one that is provided when you [import an SSH key](https://developers.google.com/apis-explorer/#p/oslogin/v1/oslogin.users.importSshPublicKey)). It's plausible that your username is not the same as your sanitized email address, so truncating at 32 characters is not a behavior I believe we should encourage.

As a note, the changes you made in your PR are modifying the accounts daemon (rather than OS Login). The accounts daemon should also validate that usernames are not longer than 32 characters, but that should be done as an update to the validation logic:
```
USER_REGEX = re.compile(r'\A[A-Za-z0-9._][A-Za-z0-9._-]*\Z')
```

> You can see from the traceback and the T4 in the dill trace tells me that
> dill is passing off the pickling of the Session class to pickle with
> save_global. For most classes, pickle can do the job, but apparently this
> is one of those cases where pickle fails on a user-built class. Since the
> return of the Session object is a problem, could you not just store it as
> self.session, or in a dict of self.sessions, and then return None or a
> string for the key for the session?
>
> Another workaround would be to add a __reduce__ method to your class, or
> to equivalently teach pickle how to reduce your class with copy_reg.
>
> Workarounds aside, this seems to be a corner case that, as far as I can
> tell, is due to your class construction more than the inability to
> serialize a Session object -- which dill should handle when asked
> directly (demonstrated previously). So what I'd say here, is that unless
> you can distill the example down to something that demonstrates it's a more
> general pickling failure, it's not something that dill should handle in
> it's code base. What I mean is that if you can construct a minimal example
> that can help demonstrate it a broader type of failure (e.g. a programming
> pattern that fails for all classes, or it's *the* primary use case in
> sqlalchemy, or something like that), then one of my suggestions above is
> probably the best path forward.

I'm curious about the motivation here... A couple of brief counterpoints:
1. Generally, the integration tests are the thing we're waiting for at the end of a run (as they're the slowest by far), so starting them later because we were waiting for the rust tests to run may actually make it take _more_ time to get a result from CI, rather than less.
2. The rust tests are really quick and easy to run locally, whereas the integration tests are super slow and hard to run locally, which means that it's much easier to fill in a missing rust result than it is a missing integration test result, if you're wanting quick feedback.

I like the general cleanup, but can you talk me through the ordering change? :)

It is typically not used, because it's basically designed to allow standby recovery exactly once, per the documentation you linked to:

```
If you're using a WAL archive, its size can be minimized using the archive_cleanup_command parameter to remove files that are no longer required by the standby server. The pg_archivecleanup utility is designed specifically to be used with archive_cleanup_command in typical single-standby configurations, see pg_archivecleanup. Note however, that if you're using the archive for backup purposes, you need to retain files needed to recover from at least the latest base backup, even if they're no longer needed by the standby.
```

In principle you can do whatever you want, but almost invariably people schedule a `wal-e delete` somehow.

I wrote a quick script which automates conversion from UTF-8 literals to stringdef.  It's quite dumb so will convert all UTF-8 it sees even if it's in comments but for your algorithm UTF-8 is only used in string literals anyway.  It also uses the full Unicode character name as the stringdef name, which is really much too verbose.  But this should provide a useful starting point - you should be able to safely do a global search and replace for a whole word to rename them for example.  Just doing a global `s/DEVANAGARI_LETTER_//g` then `s/DEVANAGARI_//g` helps a lot, but I'm not sure what the most natural character names to use here are.

Now PyDrive gets its version of oauth2client from the google-api-python-client, but is not compatible with 4.0.0 (which you can tell by the semantic versioning is not necessarily backwards compatible with 3.x.x).So, basically, PyDrive needs to compatible with oauth2client 4.0.0. Sons adds various commands to the UPnP specs.  Here is a quick script I use to dump available commands to the terminal:

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

""" Dump all known UPnP commands """

from __future__ import unicode_literals

from pprint import pprint

from soco import SoCo, discover, services

# get a zone player - any one will do
z1 = discover().pop()
print "Querying %s" % z1.player_name
# get an instance of each service
services = (srv(z1) for srv in services.Service.__subclasses__())
for srv in services:
    name = srv.service_type
    box = "="*79
    print "{0}\n|{1:^77}|\n{0}\n".format(box, name)
    for action in srv.iter_actions():
        print action.name
        print "~"*len(action.name)
        print "\n  Input"
        for arg in action.in_args:
            print "    ", arg
        print "\n  Output"
        for arg in action.out_args:
            print "    ", arg
        print "\n\n"

    # for var in srv.iter_event_vars():
    #     print "{}\n".format(var)
    print "\n\n"

```

Alternatively, I can just point at the data file and provide some examples.  Not sure if that's sufficient.  Here for example are some lines from family_name_info_p3.xml in nototools/nototools/data:

```
  <info family="sans-arab" family_name_style="short" use_preferred="t" />
  <info family="sans-arab-ui" family_name_style="very short" use_preferred="t" />
```

'family' is an identifier for a known Noto family, basically composed from 'style', 'script' and 'variant'.  We have no expectations for families whose identifiers we don't recognize-- as we add families I have to add data.  The family_name field describes how we shorten parts of the family name to fit within the limits described in the spec, this defaults to no abbreviations but can be 'short' or 'very short' as shown.  'use_preferred' is set when we have more than the four traditional variants (regular, bold, italic, bolditalic) and so want to populate the preferred family and preferred subfamily fields of the name table.  When we have more variants, they go into the standard family name field, making it longer and so typically requiring shortening of the names.  So for example we have:
```
       original_family: Noto Sans Arabic
    original_subfamily: Regular
             full_name: Noto Sans Arabic
       postscript_name: NotoSansArabic-Regular
```

but

```
       original_family: Noto Sans Arabic ExtBd
    original_subfamily: Regular
             full_name: Noto Sans Arabic ExtraBold
       postscript_name: NotoSansArabic-ExtraBold
      preferred_family: Noto Sans Arabic
   preferred_subfamily: ExtraBold
```

The 'short' version of 'ExtraBold' is 'ExtBd'.  'Short' is the value used (see the data) because for all known combinations of styles for this family (which styles depends on the family) this is sufficient to ensure they all fit.  All names in the family will follow this convention even if some would otherwise fit, so the naming across the family remains consistent.

For the UI version it's a bit different, since 'UI' appears in the name, making it longer and thus requiring more shortening of 'ExtraBold' to 'XBd':

```
       original_family: Noto Sans Arabic UI XBd
    original_subfamily: Regular
             full_name: Noto Sans Arabic UI ExtraBold
      preferred_family: Noto Sans Arabic UI
   preferred_subfamily: ExtraBold
```

Here are the short and very short names.  Currently I don't abbreviate the script names, just the style names:

```
_SHORT_NAMES = {
    'Condensed': 'Cond',
    'SemiCondensed': 'SemCond',
    'ExtraCondensed': 'ExtCond',
    'SemiLight': 'SemLt',
    'ExtraLight': 'ExtLt',
    'Medium': 'Med',
    'SemiBold': 'SemBd',
    'ExtraBold': 'ExtBd',
    'Black': 'Blk',
    'Display': 'Disp',
}

_VERY_SHORT_NAMES = {
    'Condensed': 'Cn',
    'SemiCondensed': 'SmCn',
    'ExtraCondensed': 'XCn',
    'Thin': 'Th',
    'Light': 'Lt',
    'SemiLight': 'SmLt',
    'ExtraLight': 'XLt',
    'Medium': 'Md',
    'Bold': 'Bd',
    'SemiBold': 'SmBd',
    'ExtraBold': 'XBd',
    'Black': 'Bk',
    'Display': 'D',
}
```

As I recall, I took these suggested abbreviations from a document written by Adam Twardoch.

ufo2ft forces .notdef to always be the first, so the output TTFont always has it as the first glyph. And if the font doesn't contain one, it inserts as stub glyph at that position.
There is no other sensible place for a glyph that is named ".notdef" (and of course has no codepoint assigned to it).
I believe Glyphs.app also does the same, if .notdef is present in the font.

I think that I'm going to vote 👎 on using a context manager API based on.

- The use of the object from the context manager after the context has exited seems like an anti-pattern.
- Allowing field access on the changset object after the context has exited seems to make it more likely that people will mis-use the object.
- Using a `Changeset` object in place of an RLP object likely makes using type hints harder for us.

So, I'm thinking the following now.

```python
changeset = header.build_copy(*args, **kwargs)  # creates a `Changeset` object with optional overrides from the `args/kwargs`

# you can read fields
assert changeset.number == header.number
# you can write fields
changeset.number = 5

# you can build an RLP object
do_thing_that_needs_a_header(changeset.as_serializable())  # like `commit()` but it doesn't decommission the changeset.

# you can finalize your changes
new_header = changeset.commit()
```

If a second RLP renames or reorders fields of a first, then the second RLP is not a strict subclass of the first, and we probably shouldn't encourage treating it as one. Instead, we might do something like

```py
COMMON_FIELDS = (
    ('a', big_endian_int),
    ('b', big_endian_int),
) 

class Serial1(rlp.Serializable):
    _fields = COMMON_FIELDS
 
class SerialChild(Serial1):
    """
    Appends a field. All the parent fields are present, in the same order.
    """
    _fields = ( 
        ('c', big_endian_int),
    )

class Serial2(rlp.Serializable):
    """Prepends a field"""
    _fields = (
        ('z', big_endian_int),
    ) + COMMON_FIELDS 

class Serial3(rlp.Serializable):
    """"Replaces all fields""""
    _fields = (
        ('x', big_endian_int), 
    ) 
```

In the abstract, I agree that the Django-style approach is more elegant, especially for single RLP class definitions. Since I think that's the common case (and maybe we should encourage it to be), I prefer the Django-style approach.

```py
class Serial1(rlp.Serializable):
    a = big_endian_int
    b = big_endian_int

class SerialChild(Serial1):
    """
    Appends a field. All the parent fields are present, in the same order.
    """
    c = big_endian_int

class Serial2(rlp.Serializable):
    """Prepends a field"""
    z = big_endian_int
    a = big_endian_int
    b = big_endian_int

class Serial3(rlp.Serializable):
    """"Replaces all fields""""
    x = big_endian_int
```

That little bit of repetition on `a` and `b` in `Serial2` seems worth the benefit of being to be easy to read/understand. If Serial2 is a common case, I'd love to see some real-life examples.

I probably should have mentioned that I had a chat with Jason this morning and we had the thought that it seemed nicer to just be able to register a simple string representation of a type (e.g. `"uint256"`) instead of a tuple of parsed components (e.g. `(base, sub, arrlist)`).  Regardless, it seemed like it would be nice to have a separate dictionary in which mappings from simple hashable lookups (whether strings or tuples of parsed parts) to encoders and decoders could be quickly traversed (avoiding the need to iterate through and execute a list of functions).

However, it does seem that a dictionary would work just fine to store our predicate functions since functions are hashable (as you mention) and since we require that only one applies to the type which is being looked up.  Also, we can convert simple string or tuple lookups to their equivalent predicate functions transparently.  In that case, I'd like to memoize the method on the registry class which does the lookup since it seems slow to iterate through a list of functions whenever we want to lookup an encoder or decoder.  Maybe we can clear the memo cache whenever someone registers or unregisters an encoder or decoder?

No, I think this is a bigger issue than just foisting a thing on users that they need to pin packages.  conda should do one of two things:

1. install the minimum number of things to satisfy the specified package install/update request
2. install/update everything that the package depends on to their latest versions.

I think mode 1 should be the new default behavior, and mode 2 should be achieved with a `--alldeps` flag or something.

People generally want stability.  This is especially true when people are using these tools to do real work and run production code.

In the latest version of libpcap(1.8.1 and above... see https://github.com/the-tcpdump-group/libpcap/issues/572),  the timeout parameter for pcapy_open_live doesn't cause the next function to return after waiting until timeout like in earlier version.  In 1.8.1, the next function will wait until it receives a packet and timeout is the amount of time to buffer a packet.    
The solution to the problem is to set immediate mode so it will return when not packets have been received.

I'm pretty sure the issue is that the `_NestedTransaction` `__exit__()` routine assumes that the transaction should be rolled back under all conditions. However, if an exception was raised for some non-retryable reason, the transaction is already invalidated at the server and the rollback request is met by the server's refusal to operate on the transaction any further.

If you compare the python code to the go plugin here (https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach-go/blob/master/crdb/tx.go) you will notice that if the error is not retryable they return directly without issuing a `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT` statement.

This is an unfortunate side effect of the automatic content type detection in elasticsearch itself where it tries to detect if the body contains `json` by looking at the first character.

Since this is problematic (as clearly demonstrated by this bug) it is being removed in new versions of elasticsearch where the body will have to be json and text bodies will no longer be allowed.

The fix is to send the exact body as you did here in sense, so instead of:
```
es.indices.analyze(index="publications", analyzer="standard", body="{on")
```
do:
```
es.indices.analyze(index="publications", analyzer="standard", body={"analyzer" : "standard", "text": "{on" })
```

Hope this helps

Yes in `msrestazure/azure_operation.py`, class `AzureOperationPoller` there is the following:

```
def _delay(self):
        """Check for a 'retry-after' header to set timeout,
        otherwise use configured timeout.
        """
        if self._response is None:
            return
        if self._response.headers.get('retry-after'):
            time.sleep(int(self._response.headers['retry-after']))
        else:
            time.sleep(self._timeout)
```

By default, `self._timeout` is `30` seconds.

Hi @JWDobken, thanks for reaching out!

I will certainly discuss your feature request with the team, but I think it's probably not a feature that we want to support at the moment, even though it could be very useful. The main reason is that this kind of search could be extremely expensive, and that is because the service only supports [prefix searches](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/list-blobs#uri-parameters). So for a search like `'**/reports/*.pdf'`, we basically have to list every single blob on that container (could be millions of blobs), and check on the client side if the blob name matches the pattern. If we provided this API, I'm worried that users might not realize the cost associated with the operation (you're billed by the number of I/Os after all). Nevertheless, if the service provided additional indexing/searching, then this feature is still a possibility.

So this refers to first-class support for node projects -- the goal of pre-commit is you don't need any dependencies other than installing pre-commit.  It can then handle creating isolated node / ruby / python / etc. environments without requiring the user to globally install things.  Proper node support (from pre-commit's persepective) is to make `language: node` work without dependencies.

As for your hook configuration, you're not actually depending on the node environment that pre-commit may create so you may find a `local` configuration more convenient: http://pre-commit.com/#repository-local-hooks.  Something like this

```yaml
-   repo: local
    hooks:
    -   id: eslint
        name: eslint
        entry: eslint
        language: system
        files: \.js$
```

As for the PR, yeah that sounds fine.  I think windows also does a similar prioritization.

Please allow me to retry my explanation in a much broader way I hope will be more clear. I was thinking about the philosophical design principles and the Keras design to support backends in a software engineering sense. In other words, I'm proposing we pretend Keras isn't an ML library for a moment, and focus on general API design concepts. :-) 

The guiding principles of Keras, as [François explains in the first minute of the youtube video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UeheTiBJ0Io), are also [on the top of the main keras.io page](https://keras.io/). They could, in theory, be adapted to PyBullet as follows:

- **User friendliness**. Pybullet is an API designed for human beings, not machines. It puts user experience front and center. PyBullet follows best practices for reducing cognitive load: it offers consistent & simple APIs, it minimizes the number of user actions required for common use cases, and it provides clear and actionable feedback upon user error.
- **Modularity**. A physics simulation model can be understood as either a sequence or a graph of standalone, fully-configurable modules that can be plugged together with as few restrictions as possible. In particular the renderer, linear algebra engine, physics engine, initialization schemes, Inverse Kinematics cost functions and optimizers are all standalone modules that you can combine to create new simulation models.
- **Easy extensibility**. New modules are simple to add (as new classes and functions), and existing modules provide ample examples. To be able to easily create new modules allows for total expressiveness, making PyBullet suitable for advanced physics simulation and research.
- **Work with Python**. No separate physics models, configuration files in a declarative format. Models are described in Python code, which is compact, easier to debug, and allows for ease of extensibility.

> We can already have different backends for pybullet, replacing Bullet 2.x and TinyRenderer with a different physics engine (or Bullet 3.x) and different renderer. (replacing the PhysicsServerCommandProcessor). This would require some C++ code.

Cool perhaps those backends could be selected in the same manner as they are in Keras? 

Here is an example of [keras.json](https://keras.io/backend/#kerasjson-details) adapted to a hypothetical `bullet3.json`:

```json
{
    "backend": 
     {
        "physics": "bullet3",
        "linear_algebra": "eigen3",
        "rendering": "tiny_renderer"

}
```

Plus an adapted example determining which backend is in use:

```python
# options could be specified in bullet3.json
# or before bullet3 is imported

# linalg options could be 'bullet3', 'eigen3'
>>> pybullet.linalg.backend.backend()
'bullet3'

# render options could be 'tinyrenderer', 'blender_cycles'
>>> pybullet.rendering.backend.backend()
'tinyrenderer'

# additional physics options could be tbd
>>> pybullet.physics.backend.backend()
'bullet3'
```

> I think pybullet is separate from the machine learning frameworks (Tensorflow), there is no intention to create a dependency between pybullet and Tensorflow.

I definitely agree that this is the right way to go, what I'm trying to say is that with backends the implementations for physics, linear algebra, and rendering could be selected. 

An additional side note is that the default linear algebra backend of PyBullet could create a simple graph of math operations which, when run, does those calculations with the selected backend, should it be bullet3, eigen, or something else entirely in C++ for performance reasons. The Keras backend API (with ML specific calls removed) could provide inspiration for such a mechanism. ML backends could very well be available, but this isn't necessary at all and should definitely not be a dependency.

It uses the a sPendulumGold version as comparison using this Python script:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python
from __future__ import print_function
import numpy as np
from scipy import integrate
import pylab as pl
import math
import argparse
theta_pos = 0
dtheta_pos = 1
def pendulum_system(Y, t):
  m = 10  # [kg]
  g = 9.81  # [m/s^2]
  l = 0.5  # [m]
  tau = 20  # [Nm]
  I = m * pow(l, 2)  # inertia
  return [Y[dtheta_pos], (tau - m*g*l*math.sin(Y[theta_pos])) / I]
def main():
  parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
  parser.add_argument('--plot', action='store_true', default=False, help='Plot results')
  args = parser.parse_args()
  t = np.arange(0, 2, 0.001) # [s]
  theta0 = 0
  dtheta0 = 0
  theta = integrate.odeint(pendulum_system, [theta0, dtheta0], t)  # [rad]
  print("timestamp;theta;")
  for i in range(theta.shape[0]):
    print("%f;%f;" % (t[i], theta[i,theta_pos]))
  if args.plot:
    pl.plot(t, theta[:,theta_pos], '+')
    pl.show()
if __name__ == '__main__':
  main()

```

Sorry, I guess my way to phrase things yesterday was quite, strange, indeed.

For my web application I use following approach:
```python
instance = aiohttp.web.Application()
resourceA.Controller(instance)
resourceB.Controller(instance)
```

The `Controller`-classes are derived from `aiohttp.web.Application()` and are nearly identical to the following snippet:
```python
import aiohttp.web
from aiohttp.web import route

class Conroller(aiohttp.web.Application):
    def __init__(self, parent: aiohttp.web.Application):
       super().__init__()
       
       self.router.add_routes([
           route('GET', '/some_route/', self.some_route_handler)
       ])

       if parent is not None:
           parent.add_subapp('/api/v1/', self)
```

Both controllers constructors are built up this way, only differing in the routes they register.

However, the problem is, that only the routes of the sub-application registered first are available, i.e.
```python
instance = aiohttp.web.Application()
resourceA.Controller(instance) # <- Routes of resourceA.Controller are now available
resourceB.Controller(instance) # <- Routes of resourceB.Controller are not available, yielding a HTTP 404 trying to reach them
```
When switching the order to the following, causes also that only one controllers routes are available.
```python
instance = aiohttp.web.Application()
resourceB.Controller(instance) # <- Now the routes of resourceB.Controller are available
resourceA.Controller(instance) # <- Routes of resourceA.Controller are not avaible on the contrary :-(
```

I tried to do some debugging (altough I am new to aiohttp and asynchronous io altogether). In order to verify, that all routes are being registered correctly, I iterated over every resource like this:
```python
    instance = aiohttp.web.Application()
    resourceA.Controller(instance)
    resourceB.Controller(instance)

    for route in instance.router.resources():
        print(route)
```

Judging by the output, all routes are present.

_Regarding routes that are present twice (as specified in original issue above):_
Let's pretend, that resourceA registers a get route like this
```

class Controller(aiohttp.web.Application):
    def __init__(self, parent):
        super().__init__()

        self.router.add_routes([
            route('GET','/some_route/{id:\d}', self.some_handler)
        ])

        if parent is not None:
            parent.add_subapp('/api/v1/', self) # Assume, that parent is not None here.

    @staticmethod
    async def some_handler(request: aiohttp.web.Request):
        return aiohttp.web.Response()
```

If we were to loop over the resources registered to the main application (`instance`), I'd get the following result:
> <DynamicResource  /api/v1/some_route/{id}
> <DynamicResource  /api/v1/some_route/{id}

Which strongly suggests, that the very same route, that was added only once, is present twice in the main application's resource table. However, I am not sure, wether this is intended behavior.

The kernel started for a notebook has a working directory of where the notebook file is. I think that makes sense for a *lot* of usecases, and is immediately understandable.

If you want to be able to import python files from another place, you could add that other directory to your sys.prefix. You could even put this into a small stub python file in the notebook directory and import it at the top of each notebook. For example, put this in `myfiles.py` in the same directory as the notebooks:

```python
import sys
import os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), '../python')))
```
and then in the notebook, just do `import myfiles`. That will set up the path so that you can import your python files.

Hi all, a few thoughts (took a couple days to get my bearings on this one as there are a few related issues with which I was getting it confused). My unraveling this confusion led to these different points. 

1. this seems (based on the exposition…I've not tried testing this yet) to address an issue I opened ages ago: https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/759. If that is the scope of that, I think it should be fine. 

2. Technically the use of `\\( …\\)` inside the notebook isn't "escaping" the `\` so that it can be interpreted by the underlying javascript, but it is specifically the syntax we use in contrast to traditional `\(` `\)`. I think we made an explicit decision to not support those as delimiters (see next point). I don't think that this is actually doing that (based on the number of `\`s that are present in the various instances of the commit). That said, that regular expression is a beast to read and not test, so I'll need a little time to error test it thoroughly. 

2.1. This should probably have some accompanying tests to check for the previously aberrant behaviour (in https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/759 and https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/980), but that assumes that we're still expanding our test suite. In other cases (e.g., https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/pull/2048) it was decided that tests weren't required. This seems like a particularly precarious case (as it's a "bugfix" rather than a "feature request") and so if there _is_ any planned further expansion of our test suite, then this would merit inclusion.

3. If this _is_ allowing `\(` `\)` as delimiters (nb: I don't think it is), can someone explain why this is ok but other PRs that seemed to try to do something similar weren't (e.g., https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/pull/1935 &  https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/issues/477 )? I'm not opposed to including it in theory, but it strictly speaking is a departure from the the commonmark approach as quoted previously by @rgbkrk: 

> [the problem with that syntax is that `\[` and `\(` already have clearly defined meanings: they are escaped `[` and `(` characters, and it is very important that there be a way to escape these characters in certain circumstances](https://talk.commonmark.org/t/mathematics-extension/457/17)

I think this observation is unrelated to this PR beyond from clarifying a point about when certain syntaxes are valid or invalid. However, if we wanted to allow `\[` after two new lines (i.e., `\n\n\\\[`, a `\[` with only a blank above it…) which I believe is currently needed for the `\begin{` syntax to be detected, that could be fine. If you wanted to get back the `\[` escapement, then you just include a space in the previous line, nbd. But, I'm hard pressed to think of a way to allow `\(` and the escapement of `(` at the same time (since it is intended to be "inline" mathematics no whitespace can be used to distinguish it cleanly).

@cassiusoat's note about mouse and trackpad might be an important clue. macOS can hide scroll bars until scrolling starts. I believe the default (certainly an option) is to hide by default when using a gesture-enabled trackpad, but always show scroll bars if a mouse is in use. If CodeMirror is measuring assuming a scroll bar would always be present, but it isn't, we could get this behavior (cutoff of the width of one scroll bar). To enable this behavior explicitly: System Preferences > General > Show scroll bars: When scrolling It's possible it's specifically an interacting with the 'When scrolling' setting. It's also possible that it's related to switching back and forth in the "Automatically..." state, where CM makes a decision based on prior state, but doesn't update if that state changes.

**Hazarded explanation:**
I'm guessing when `tox` calls `pip` with `PYTHONPATH` defined, `pip` sees the package as already installed at the current version, and so it doesn't actually install the newly built dist into the virtualenv, even though it doesn't actually exist there.  Then, for whatever internal reason, when `tox` calls python` within the virtualenv, the `python` instance doesn't have visibility to the `PYTHONPATH` env variable in the outer scope, and so the `import opan.test` call fails with the `ImportError`.

Using a dual number type is a slick way to implement forward-mode automatic differentiation. However, autograd implements reverse-mode automatic differentiation, which is generally more efficient for functions R^n -> R^m when n > m. Since in optimization we usually want to differentiate objective functions which look like R^n -> R, maybe with very large n when neural nets are involved, reverse-mode autodiff is generally preferred.

Let me take a bit of the guesswork out here:
The data table reload takes milliseconds, that is not a problem. 
The issue (AFAICT) is 3 fold: 
  - If you're running galaxy using the default config in run.sh you're just using a single shared web and job handler process, so no cross-process communication is necessary to notify all processes that new data table entries are available. Installing tools / data managers and running them is not a problem. The manual reloading through the API is not necessary.
  - In a production setup with multiple processes the process that received the job (ie. running the data manager) will get the new table -- if you're just using 1 process that will be fine, but most setups use multiple processes and threads, these need to be notified somehow. We can do this 2 ways 
    -  each process can listen to changes to the data tables, or we can use an external messaging queue like rabbitmq to pass messages. @bgruening's image comes with rabbitmq, but it isn't setup properly, and maintaining rabbitmq is another additional complication, so that's not ideal.
    - we use watchdog to monitor the individual tables in each process, which means watchdog must be installed and `watch_tool_data_dir` must be set. This works fine (I've been using this in production ever since I added this option), but it only listens to tables that are known during server startup. So if you install a new data manager, but you don't restart galaxy watchdog will not listen to the new table.

For both of the latter option it appears as if hitting the data table endpoint works if you just do it often enough, because you stochastically hit the different processes/threads, but the handlers will never be updated, so when you run a job that relies on a dbkey and a fasta item, the job will simply fail, which is what we've been observing before https://github.com/galaxyproject/ephemeris/pull/53/commits/ed6a30883d89cde7c06cad7a763d9d1dbc29f111.
To not give people reading the code the wrong idea, I'd be strongly in favor of removing `tool_data_client.reload_data_table(str(data_table))`

Okay, I can work on this, but I don't have access to a Mac at the moment, and OSX doesn't play nicely (legally) in a VM, even with a license I believe.

I'd also note that I like the idea of non-expiring names only for usernames. Domain names definitely should have a reasonable expiration period (1 year, more or less).

Another way to look at this is that user ID numbers should be long lasting, but usernames should be changeable. This is how Facebook and Twitter work. You get a permanent ID number you can't ever change. But you can always change your username. It's for this reason that I kind of like @ChristopherA's idea of having 1 namespace for registering user ID #'s and 1 namespace for mapping usernames to these ID #'s.

That said, this greatly overcomplicates things and it should be avoided.

Surfacing a suggestion I've mentioned in conversation and on Slack: https://github.com/kevin1024/vcrpy would be a good way to test this on Travis-CI without actually having to install the Archiver on Travis-CI. For those who don't already know, VCR works like this:

1. Stand up a working server _locally_ once. In our case, we could use the actual Archiver running at NSLS-II.
2. Run the tests. For every request that occurs during the test, VCR captures the client's request and the corresponding response from the server. It records this information in JSON files that are intended to be added to version control.
3. Tear down the server (or switch to a machine that can't see the server).
4. Run the tests again. VCR magically mocks the server's response to each client request.

Thus, there is no need to get the server working on Travis-CI. Of course, if a test issues a request that VCR has not seen before, VCR blows up and the test fails, but you are free to _refactor_ the tests without re-generating the VCR files.

It has to be done recursively: If the input list is 10 samples, spawn concrete pipeline children each one of them. Otherwise, partition the list into 10 sublists and add a child with the current function for each of the sublists.

Ideally this should have been the first thing to do because any problem that happens during the migration process (and believe me, they happen) will require the process to be restarted from the beginning, resulting in "stale" issues because GitHub does not allow issues to be deleted. In this case, the solution is to delete the repository and try again. Once you are satisfied on how the issues were migrated, you can continue with the rest of the migration process (code, links, etc). 

At least that was my experience with migrating `pytest` to GitHub.

@mvantellingen Merged in master again. `TestScales.test_decimals` was broken. 

Basically, the weight attribute is a `float` (and there is no decimal type), so when the scale weighs 3 items that weight `0.3`, it ends up with `3 * Decimal(0.3)` which not the same as `Decimal('0.9')` due to float approximation. One solution is to quantize the decimal result with the `Decimal.quantize` method to round the weight to the nearest tenths (as I have done here). Another solution would be to use `self.assertAlmostEqual` instead, but I thought that was less clear.
We shall also mention about this in the release docs.

pde and I have been thinking about this issue a lot over the past couple of days and I wanted to provide my thoughts.

For starters, I don't think doing this should involve two client invocations. That is first suggestion that pde posted. Doing this feels clunky, would require some major changes in the client, and I can't think of anything you could do in this approach that you couldn't do with passing commands/scripts to the client.

Next, between the options of passing one or two scripts to the client, I think I like the option of two scripts better. If we put everything in one large script, we have to have a LE defined way of separating out the deploy and cleanup code (such as the `bash` functions with specific names). This seems more complicated and less intuitive than just having separate deploy/cleanup commands/scripts.

Furthermore, as suggested by pde, having the client take two commands still provides a way to write a single script for the manual plugin that can be easily shared with others. The large script can define bash functions, create temporary files, etc.

Lastly, I'd like to suggest that we don't provide multiple ways to do this, unless there is a real benefit. We could provide something like `--manual-deploy-command`, `--manual-cleanup-command`, and `--manual-combined-command`, but by doing this we further complicate our command line and I'm unaware of anything you could do with one approach that you couldn't do with the other.

If anyone else has thoughts on this, I'd love to hear them. If this is done right, I think it could be a huge benefit to our users with more complex server setups.

This should have worked already - sites-enabled should be parsed because it's "Include":d from apache2.conf in the root directory, and if for some reason it was not, it can be parsed by providing --apache-vhost-root /etc/apache2/sites-enabled in the command line, provided by #1811. 

However in some cases (and especially if defined in the command line) it was NOT being parsed, because of libaugeas bug, that prevents parsing of overlapping match definitions. #2092 fixes that problem, by handling the augeas parsing paths if they overlap. Leaving only the "wildest card" match rule intact

Just went through the same issue (though, with Go client.
There were 2 versions in my system:

1. `/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/librdkafka.so.1`
2. `/usr/local/lib/librdkafka.so.1`

It turned out,  the binary generated was loading first.
```
lafolle@yoss:~/src/kafkatest/cmd/pro$ ldd pro
        linux-vdso.so.1 =>  (0x00007ffda9758000)
        librdkafka.so.1 => /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/librdkafka.so.1 (0x00007f9786d56000)
        libpthread.so.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpthread.so.0 (0x00007f9786b38000)
```

Solution was to remove first,  and set LD_LIBRARY_PATH to `/usr/local/lib` and rebuild.

> Without looking to deep it might be the case that _send_request() only returns values in the 2xx range, in which case that if-statement should be removed instead. Needs further investigation.

That appears to be false. `_send_request()` simply uses the `requests` library to make an HTTP call.
The `requests` library itself does not raise errors if the status code is not 2xx, but rather simply returns the response object.
```
import requests
print(requests.get('https://httpstat.us/200').status_code) # => 200
print(requests.get('https://httpstat.us/400').status_code) # => 400
```

    mapper = model.__mapper__

        for column in mapper.primary_key

Your opinion of this being "relatively simple" is not something that Z3 can use to solve the problem quickly. There are lots of quantifiers and patterns in the query, as well as multiple theories ("hard ones" like arrays, datatypes, strings), so it may easily get lost instantiating useless things or the search heuristics send it down a slow path. The output is expected: it tells you that it's searching, trying lots of different things. If the output stops for a long time there may be a bug that causes non-termination, but that doesn't seem to be the case here. 

If your intuition about this being "relatively simple" can be formalized in some way, then we can add that information to the query (e.g. better patterns, some specific pre-processing, etc). The string theory is also relatively new, so that may be a good thing to look at first, where the theories are concerned.

It's a good point. Z3's functions follows pretty much what the SMT-LIB2 efforts around strings are taking. There is str.at/seq.at, but it returns a string/sequence of length 1. My understanding is that the SMT-LIB2 efforts preferred to keep character types opaque. So accessing a character code would break this.

In Z3 there is one way to break the abstraction: Strings are sequences of 8 bit characters.
You can then define a relation and use it for accessing the character code.

    (define-fun char_at ((s String) (index Int) (ch (_ BitVec 8))) Bool
              (= (str.at s index) (seq.unit ch)))

    (declare-const s0 String)
    (declare-const i0 Int)
    (declare-const ch0 (_ BitVec 8))
    (assert (char_at s0 i0 ch0))
    (assert (<= 0 i0))
    (assert (<= i0 (str.len s0)))
    (check-sat) 
    (get-model)

If you want a function (charCodeAt), then for every occurrence of that function (charCodeAt s1 i1) add the assertion 

            (=> (and (<= 0 i1) (< i1 (str.len s1)) (char_at s1 i1 (charCodeAt s1 i1))

leg2 = InputLayer(second_input_shape)
leg2 = DenseLayer(leg2, num_units=whatever, nonlinearity=None, b=None)
joined = ElemwiseSumLayer([leg1, leg2])
joined = NonlinearityLayer(joined, nonlinearity=sigmoid)
joined = DenseLayer(joined, num_units=whatever_else, nonlinearity=softmax)
```
Equivalently, instead of separating the two dot products (`W1*X1` and `W2*X2`), you can combine them into one:
```
from lasagne.layers import ConcatLayer
leg1 = InputLayer(first_input_shape)
leg2 = InputLayer(second_input_shape)
joined = ConcatLayer([leg1, leg2])
joined = DenseLayer(joined, num_units=whatever, nonlinearity=sigmoid)
joined = DenseLayer(joined, num_units=whatever_else, nonlinearity=softmax)
```
By the way, your example had a softmax directly on a sigmoid, I think that's not what you wanted (so I omitted the last sigmoid).

In future, please post such questions on our mailing list: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/lasagne-users
This issue tracker is meant for bug reports and feature discussions. Thank you!

Since wagtail 1.5.x it is possible to "solve" this issue with a wagtail-hook.

Create a wagtail_hooks.py file in one of your app directories and add the following code:

```
from __future__ import unicode_literals

from wagtail.wagtailcore import hooks

@hooks.register('construct_explorer_page_queryset')
def show_translated_pages(parent_page, pages, request):
    """
    Display page titles in explorer list view in session language by using 
    specific Page models instead of wagtailcore.Page models.
    """
    return pages.specific()
```

With this hook page titles in explorer list views will be displayed in an editor's current session language.
We fixed this in version 0.6.0 but we tried to make the flow as similar as possible to the get_edit_handler of wagtail. Anyway, thanks for your contribution.

Obviously, this isn't very general (i.e., you might not know the size of the file ahead of time, or want to do this for a general size of the time).

My preference would be to keep SimFile simple and, instead, put the logic to handle short reads into the read syscall (https://github.com/angr/simuvex/blob/master/simuvex/procedures/syscalls/read.py). As to *how* to do this in a performant way, I'm not quite sure yet. We can create a new symbolic variable, constrain it to be less than or equal to the provided size, then pass *that* into SimFile.read and return it. This would have the unfortunate effect of causing a path explosion for every `read_exactly_n` type loop, though.

@rob-c There are a lot of cases there and a lot to take in but I'll try and address them

> `1.` Objects on disk are a subset of what is required by the Schema.

In this case as you say we would load the object and the load would succeed as all attributes would load properly and defaults would naturally fill the rest. It is then an option we have to compare how many we loaded to how many there should be and warn the user of the new defaults.

> `2.` Objects on disk are a superset of what is required by the Schema.

Without the version information I would foresee that this would fail as an attribute read from disk could not be set on the current object  so loading fails and the user is warned. This object is incompatible with the new version and the user is told they cannot load this object. This I think makes sense as there is clearly extra information that the new version doesn't know how to handle as so cannot guarantee that the object will be treated the same as it was in the old version.

> `3.` How do we know if it's safe to load a job if the Schema has changed?

If it loads it's safe. This means that the onus is on tightening up the schema attributes themselves. This can include adding a list of allowed types/values.


> `4.` If we assume the Schema is always compatible with the information stored on disk then we should be explicitly allowed to load it for monitoring etc:

I'm afraid I didn't fully understand this. I would say we never assume that an object on disk is compatible and as above if it loads then it is compatible.


> `5.` If we explicitly define that any change in the Schema are non-compatible should we lock the job and warn the user?

I don't think that we explicitly define this for example if my attribute is defined as taking the values 'a' and 'b' and then I change the schema to allow 'a', 'b' and 'c' then this new schema will load the old object perfectly well and it's compatible.


> __Bad example__
> e.g:
> 1. myApp in Ganga v7r1 creates a job which requires a certain application configurable to know where data is. The default value corresponds to eos.
> 2. myApp in Ganga v7r2 no longer supports eos but the schema exposed to the user doesn't know this.
> 3. myApp from Ganga v7r1 is loaded with v7r2 and fails due to now being able to cope with eos storage.
> 4. Ganga v7r2 writes the job back to disk and Ganga v7r1 now has a problem loading the job correctly due to some minor schema change.

In this case as step 2 when the object is being loaded it will fail as eos is no longer valid. so this object is incompatible and will not load in the new version which I think is what we want as it is true that eos is not valid.


> If the schema is assumed to be compatible:
> 
>     Ganga v7r1 creates a job
>     Ganga v7r2 loads the job and appends the schema and writes it to disk
>     Ganga v7r1 is now used by the user because they didn't/couldn't use v7r2. How does Ganga v7r1 know what the changed Schema is compatible?
>     Ganga v7r1 attempts to load a newer job from Ganga v7r2 and mangles the job as the new schema attribute changed how other objects in the schema are used.

In this case if the new schema from v7r2 is incompatible with v7r1 it will not load in the old version.  if it is then it will load.


> If the schema change is assumed to be non compatible:
> 
>     Ganga v7r1p0 creates a job
>     Ganga v7r1p1 modified the job definition to allow for the user to configure some behaviour.
>     Ganga v7r1p1 cannot load a job from Ganga v7r1p0 even though Ganga v7r1p0 is being deprecated and has a known bug.
>     After many cycles of this users don't know which version of Ganga is safe to use.

Sorry I don't understand this one. if v7r1p1 loads and modifies the job (which amounts to adding defaults to missing items.) then it has loaded the job from v7r1p0

Somehow this message got deleted from this thread, but I'll answer anyway: there is a major difference between requesting users to cite your paper(s), or requiring them to do so. The former is good academic practice anyway - you should cite software you use just like you cite relevant papers. The latter basically means that your software does not qualify as open source software, and cannot be used in any mainstream package. 

So I'd strongly suggest sticking with a standard 3-clause BSD license, and *requesting* users to cite a particular paper.

> We've debated it numerous times internally. The factory calibration toolset is huge -- easily 100k lines of C++ just for R200 alone. It's not maintainable as an open-source project, so it will never be included as part of librealsense.

As I said, I don't trust what I see with the calibration app as it currently is... I'm not even sure if the depth distortion model of the RealSense devices is similar to that of Kinect, so for many applications, there's just not enough information about the underworkings of the sensor so that we can proceed with building our applications using sound assumptions. ```
        if hasattr(original_error, 'stack'):
            stack = original_error.stack
```
should check `original_error.__traceback__` instead, because `original_error` may be just an arbitrary Python exceptions which might not have `stack` property (which is the convention in graphql-core). But `__traecback__` is only available in Python 3, so we need some other way to do the same for Python 2.

For those interested, the issue can be solved by saving the module into GCP, you can do it with the following steps:

On some local machine
1. [optional] clear all modules: `rm -fr /tmp/tfhub_modules`
2. Run some code that downloads the module: `hub.Module(...)`
3. Upload the module to some gcp bucket: `gsutil -m cp /tmp/tfhub_modules/{module_hash} gs://bucket/some/path/to/module`

Now, on you code you can use pass the location of the bucket to TF Hub
```python
hub.Module("gs://bucket/some/path/to/module")
```

@chrisranderson I think the project you described is very interesting and can be very useful for a lot of people. It will benefit model interpretation, understanding and debugging, which is getting more and more important as new types of DL models get invented every week. TensorFlow has TensorBoard and TFDBG, both of which has limitations. For example, TFDBG allows you to see all the intermediate tensor values during runtime. But all it currently has is a text-based interface in the shell, which is not ideal for visualizing the graph structures in TensorFlow models. TensorBoard has great graph visualization, but its connection with the TensorFlow runtime is not real-time. A visual debugger for TensorFlow in TensorBoard will be a great feature. Just imagine what you can see and do if you could "step" through nodes of a graph, visualize its output tensor as a table, a curve, an image or a video. You can also modify the tensor value before continuing further on the graph... 

TFDBG already has a protocol for real-time streaming of data from TF runtime. But as dandelion@ and jart@ pointed out, due to some yet-unfulfilled feature requests in the gRPC library, these are not fully functional in open-source tensorflow yet. The C++ libraries of the aforementioned protocol is not fully open-source yet, but I can easily make them open-source soon.

I'll think twice before implementing the protocol again in another framework, as it may cause unnecessary duplicate work and confusion to clients.

I can check with the gRPC team on their time line to fulfill the feature request. The request mainly has to do with implementing a py_grpc_library bazel genrule. Even if their timeline is too far in the future, we can find a way to bypass the missing feature and do it the same way as the way tensorflow/core/distributed_runtime does it, i.e., implement the server in C++. The part we have to work out ourselves is SWIG-wrapping it so that it can be used in Python, as a TensorBoard plugin.

Per offline discussion with @caisq, we will later remove the health pills in the graph dashboard following an announcement. Otherwise, we surprise the current users of that feature.

Hence, I am closing this PR.

I see the point, and I think it definitely falls into hotdoc's scope as a documentation compiler. I have in my future plans the writing of a git extension, which could also do smart things such as letting us show the last version where a file was modified in apertis designs. As I see it, hotdoc core would provide the check mechanisms (is this comment appropriately tagged?), and the git extension would provide the version, leaving the door open for someone using a different version control system to write and use a different extension.

If you're interested in helping with this, we could share the work as follows:

I would implement a "discovering_version" signal in hotdoc, emitted if the version was not provided from the command-line or a configuration file (through `project-version`).

You would implement an extension (that's very simple), which would connect to this signal : `doc_repo.discovering_version_signal.connect` and return a tuple `(project_version, is_release)`.

Then, upon creation of a comment, hotdoc could trivially check `if since.lower() == "unreleased": warn(...)`.

This is only a warning, but can be promoted to an error either through `fatal-warnings` or through a yet to be implemented error-code-selective argument (all warnings already have codes).

The code for this extension could then be extended further to take advantage of more information from git.

Apart from that I would like to improve the style of parameters. Currently we have method-parameters like `type="BOLD"`. I would prefer something like `type=texttype.bold `. @ktbyers your point regarding state tracking is fair. This can be made safer using a method decorator that implements the `enable; do a file operation; enable` logic rather than having separate methods that could be called out of order. There is no way to track that state across runs if we encounter (for example) transport errors during `do a file operation` though.
Providing a kwarg to `__init__` is a non-starter for me, as one would need to call the constructor differently depending on the desired final config of the managed device, which rather defeats the whole purpose of using Napalm in the first place.
I have no problem with making `file prompt quiet` a pre-requisite, and agree that this is probably the simplest option. The downside to this is that people may have existing tooling (expect scripts, etc) that assumes file operations *will* be prompted for, and this would break things for those people.
In general config pre-reqs should not be enabled auto-magically, nor removed silently - i.e. they should all be handled like the `archive` requirement.
It would be great if we could agree on a solution to this as soon as possible though, as I am currently having to run my fork in production :-(

Hi, thanks for reporting the issue!

There is a strange behavior here, 
```
self.tensors = to_list(tensors)
self.graph = tensors[0].graph
```
to_list should ensure that tensors is a list... thus tensors[0] should be defined (or throw an index exception if None). 'tensors' is a list of tensors (because Evaluator can evaluate multiple tensors at the same time). In case the user send a single Tensor, then tensors is converted to an array using 'to_list' (and that array then only containing a single tensor). But here, it seem it didn't work.

As a fix, changing load function of generator class (https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/models/generator.py#L215) to will fix that issue:
```
def load(self, model_file):
        """ Load.

        Restore model weights.

        Arguments:
            model_file: `str`. Model path.

        """
        self.trainer.restore(model_file)
        self.session = self.trainer.session
        self.predictor = Evaluator([self.net],
                                   session=self.session)
```

But furthermore investigation are needed to find out why to_list haven't converted the Tensor to a `list` of Tensor.

> With this PR, the headers are placed into PYTHON_INCLUDE_DIRS/pybind11 while the CMake config files are going into CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX. This isn't going to work because CMake expects that the headers and config files are placed consistently relative to CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX. The find_package tests are failing because they're testing expected CMake behavior and I don't think this should be modified.
> A better way to do this would be to just give CMake a different CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX so that everything is still consistent. But this isn't compatible with the current way pybind11's setup.py installs headers into include/pythonx.x/pybind11. An alternative would be to adopt a numpy-style header location: site-packages/pybind11/include. The nice thing about this is that the CMake config files could also be installed into site-packages/pybind11/share/cmake. On the CMake side, the change then becomes trivial: CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=site-packages/pybind11 just needs to be passed as an argument. This would keep everything nice and consistent. I was thinking about proposing this, but the only downside is that it would complicate setup.py and potentially add cmake as an install dependency.
> 
> Note that switching the header install location from include/pythonx.x/pybind11 to site-packages/pybind11/include would also resolve this PyPy issue: #596.

Actually even simpler is to make both user=True and =False return the same correct path...

Suggested "correct" implementation:
```
_SENTINEL = object()


def get_include(user=_SENTINEL):
    import os
    import re
    import subprocess
    import sys
    import warnings

    if user is not _SENTINEL:
        warnings.warn("Passing 'user' to get_include is deprecated.",
                      DeprecationWarning)
    lines = subprocess.check_output(
        [sys.executable, "-mpip", "show", "-f", "pybind11"],
        universal_newlines=True).splitlines()
    loc_match, = filter(
        None, (re.match(r"\ALocation: (.*)\Z", line) for line in lines))
    header_match, = filter(
        None, (re.match(r"\A  (.*\bpybind11.h)\Z", line) for line in lines))
    loc = loc_match.group(1)
    header = header_match.group(1)
    return os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.join(loc, header)))
```

This PR reverts #4248 which pinned a newer version of `requests` that provided better error messages for `SSLErrors` that occur. Since our conversation in #4433 though where we decided that we're only actively supporting installations through OS packages or `certbot-auto` (or Docker) though, I don't think this pinning buys us anything. `certbot-auto` pins an earlier version and OS packages will use whatever they have available. Perhaps this pinning would have caused an OS to upgrade their `requests` version, however, there's no guarantee that will actually happen and removing it allows easy testing with older versions to prevent regressions in systems like CentOS/RHEL which I think is more important.

> >   Some new users think that "renew" does not replace the certificate, just somehow automagically  extend the validity of the existing certificate.
>
> This is an intentional design choice. Our goal is to create an abstraction, so that most users don't need to know that renew does not automagically extend the validity of the existing certificate.

I completely agree that it's nice to a have an automagically working, 'push-button" "appliance" that you don't need to tune.  Then you can work with it as a "black-box" abstraction.
But  due to the variation of the systems and setups, people need to introduce changes, and that where a misunderstanding can lead to a disaster. One specific example: A user thought that he/she can **move** (not copy!) the certificates to a different server, while the renewal process would be happening from the initial server (and was obviously surprise why the renewal did not happen). I hope this example shows clearly why _understanding_ of the "magic" is often important.
However, while this question is interesting and important, here, I used it only to illustrate where some (sometimes very strange sounding) misunderstandings stem from.


Now, let's ~~encry...~~  focus on the _essential topics_:
You asked very good questions.
Let me start with the simple answer first:
- Re: deprecation: yes, I understand that the backward compatibility is important. And in this case, indeed hiding --expand should be fine. What I actually had in mind is along the lines of what is typically written in such cases:
"The older syntax is still supported for backwards-compatibility reasons, but has been deprecated and may go away in the future."

> - Do you imagine modify is an exact stand-in for renew?
- No, it is not a substitute. From the user's perspective it **must** be very different, and that why I actually proposed to use `modify` here (with --add-domain and --delete-domain) **instead** of renew.
(This will become more clear in a few moments, when I will describe my "broad-scale" view of certbot after answering the questions first.)

> - If a cert is not due fore renewal, should calling modify get a new cert anyway?
- Yes! 
(In principle, one can add a verification here, but I would argue that from the logic of `modify`, as I am proposing it, that verification would be counter-intuitive.)

> - How would you imagine these flags affecting certonly/run commands?
- You mostly answered it yourself: They are very different and independent. So, the answer is "No".


###  -----------------------------------

Let me now share with you you I see certbot on a "broad-scale". This is not necessarily how things are implemented under the hood, but how they are seen from the user's perspective, and from the use cases. (I actually started drafting this earlier as a separate issue, but it looks like its time to bring it up here).

Let me first quote an excerpt from a recent irc conversation with @osirisinferi (I hope he doesn't mind me quoting him):
```
<OsirisInferi> ... From the user's perspective, there are actually 3 modes of operation 
                      of certbot: 
<OsirisInferi> A) Initialize a new lineage with one or more domains
<OsirisInferi> B) Modify a current lineage
<OsirisInferi> C) Renewal 
```
I actually agree with that point of view, but with a few modes added:
D) revoke (cert[s])
E) account management (register, modify and un-register account,)
F) manage locally [certs and server configs locally] (install, delete, update_symlinks, [view] certificates .... , config_changes, rollback)

The first 4, A-D, are 4 modes that work on certs with the CA server. 
E) does account management.
F) does local cert and server config management 

In principle, F) consists of two parts: manage certs and manage server configs [both .locally]. I am not sure if separating makes it more clear or not.

I think this classification and separation makes a much clearer picture for anybody who starts using certbot. And I think it makes my responses above to @ohemorange 's questions about `modify` vs. `renew` clear (yet without any specific explanations), right? And  it becomes clear why "modify" does not really need a confirmation for non-expiring certs (well, because you asked to **modify**).

This classification/separation helps clearing all sort of confusions or ambiguities. E.g., do you need `renew` with --lineage, if you want to remove one of the domains from the existing cert? (Or should it be nothing, because _nothing_ implicitly stands for `run`?)
[Whoever was the author of the initial cli with `renew`, `run`, please don't take it wrong: What you've done is great. I am just trying to share with you what most of the core team have never experienced: how the current cli looks to people learning about certbot from scratch.)]

I have more thoughts on this, but let me stop here for now,
I'd like to hear feedback on what I described above, and I would be happy to address questions if any.

I've debugged it in Wireshark a bit. I've spotted very strange things. The proxy answers to Upgrade request **immediately** with HTTP 101 "Switching protocol", before it even sends the request to the backend! Probably I'm bad at asynchronous programming, but it looks too asynchronous for me.

In details, when I connect directly, HTML5 client switches protocol to websocket and sends "hello" packet to the Xpra server:

1. Browser -> Server: Upgrade to WebSocket
2. Server -> Browser:  HTTP 101 Switching protocol
3. Browser -> Server: WebSocket: Hello message with client details
4. Server -> Browser: answer to the hello, communication starts

Behind the proxy, the "hello" message never gets to the backend:

1. Browser -> Proxy: Upgrade to WebSocket
2. Proxy -> Browser: HTTP 101 Switching protocol — haven't even asked the backend!!!
3. Proxy -> Backend: Upgrade to WebSocket
4. Browser -> Proxy: WebSocket: Hello message with client details — Proxy sends it to /dev/null
5. Backend -> Proxy: HTTP 101 Switching protocol
6. (10 seconds later) Browser -> Proxy: WebSocket connection close — never got answer to Hello request
7. Proxy -> Backend: WebSocket connection close — at lest "connection close" was not redirected to /dev/null, thanks!

When working on https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/1858 I thought of the same problem.

My 2 cents:

> The user uses the proxies directly from the console

I would not worry about this case as this is not supported. The console in general is just for us and other hardcore people. "Here Be Dragons".

> Some of the REST APIs use the respective methods from RaidenAPI which are not state machine aware.

Which ones are these?


> The proxies are used on a raiden event handler.
> But, for the second scenario, the state of the raiden node will be changed, and we cant recover.

Yes this is the troubling case as you describe in your example.

> The Raiden node should quit

That can cause them to lose funds as they would no longer be online to monitor channels. On the other hand, even if the node stays online but has no ETH to go on-chain in case of a dispute it will still lose money.

> A thread must wait until the account has enough ether to pay for the transaction, and then the transaction must be retried 

This would be the best solution in a combination with strategies designed to keep the raiden node with enough ETH.

We should somehow warn the user if he is about to:
- make sure he has enough funds (and then some more for dispute) for all his transactions with the current gas price before joining a token network.
-  run out of funds for transactions
- does not have enough funds to close and settle all of his channels (so to perform the `leave_network` action)

How to warn? Emitting warning messages in the log and a pop up in the webui are the first things I can think of.

> So for example, say that a message is not sent after all 5 retries, what do you think we should do?

The node should wait for a Ping message from the partner before trying again, so when a node comes up it needs to send a Ping to each participant for notifying them that the node is online and checking who is online.

We have decided to prioritize https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1686 for now, and to revisit this in again in a couple months.

Over in https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws/issues/249, @rgwood said

> By the way, is requiring the compiled JS output like require('./bin/lambda'); the recommended approach for multi-file TypeScript projects like this? Not the end of the world but it feels a little awkward to reference compiled output instead of importing the Typescript class like import * as lambda from "./lambda"; (when I do that I still get a serialization error).

I have a feeling all of this is related.  It looks like

* when `import` is used, we see the import, attempt to serialize it, and fail
* when `require` is used, we somehow miss the import, and so don't even attempt to serialize it

Of course, in the latter case, we fail at runtime, because the runtime dependency is missing.

The reason we fail to serialize is because the `uuid` module uses native code:

```
              module './node_modules/uuid/index.js' which indirectly referenced
                function 'v4': v4.js(4,11): which captured
                  module './node_modules/uuid/lib/rng.js' which indirectly referenced
                    function 'nodeRNG': rng.js(6,33): which captured
                      module 'crypto' which indirectly referenced
                        function 'randomBytes': random.js(52,20): which referenced
                          function 'assertSize': random.js(33,19): which captured
                            'ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE', a function defined at
                              function 'NodeError': errors.js(156,15): which referenced
                                function 'getMessage': errors.js(208,19): which captured
                                  variable 'messages' which indirectly referenced
                                    function 'get': which could not be serialized because
                                      it was a native code function.
```

In summary, this is particularly unfortunate for a few reasons

1. why did we miss the capture if you resort to `require`? 
2. `crypto` is a built-in Node.js module, why did we even attempt to serialize it?
3. I think we should strongly reconsider reifying captures as `require`s at runtime

so @skilleek method works for the time being, you don't need to download livestreamer at all though. 

what you can do is download the script from his link and put it in the plugins folder for streamlink (Default for me was "C:\Program Files (x86)\Streamlink\pkgs\streamlink\plugins"), rename the old plugin to vaughnlive.py.bak (or just replace the old script with the one from the script above) and then go into the new script and replace 
```
from livestreamer.plugin import Plugin
from livestreamer.plugin.api import http, validate
from livestreamer.stream import RTMPStream
```
to 
```
from streamlink.plugin import Plugin
from streamlink.plugin.api import http, validate
from streamlink.stream import RTMPStream
```

You replace `livecli` with `streamlink`, which should mostly work. I believe @back-to has abandoned that project in favour of `streamlink` and a custom plugins repo - which probably contains compatible versions of the plugins you want. 

Yes, it checks all the plugins - that’s the only way it knows if the url you give it is supported and by which plugin. Not sure what you mean by politically correct, they just have to be correct ;) You can also remove plugins that you do not want. However, the effect will be pretty negligible (unless you are running on some embedded system, which it doesn’t look like). Any file reads for loading plugins is handled by the python implementation that you are using and is not something that is directly part of streamlink.

With greatest respect, this issue **ISN'T SOLVED**, hence it shouldn't have been closed!
If you read (again) the very first post of this thread, you'll realise it's a case of what I described/explained further down [here](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/1436#issuecomment-357735759)... 
**TL;DR**: 
Not all ITVhub shows (Video-On-Demand) are available as RTMPE streams (compatible with streamlink's itvplayer plugin); but all (including those that are failing with SL) are now available as AppleHLS streams (that the current iteration of the plugin can't intercept). 

 In addition to the above for VOD, ITV are also deprecating their **live** RTMPE streams for the group of their channels (currently only one variant @1200k available for each of them - used to be five, the best @1800k). 
Recent tests on desktop Firefox reveal that only ITV1+ITV4 (live) still employ Flash and RTMPE streams, while now ITV2+ITV3 live streams are of the encrypted MPEG-DASH type (with Widevine CDM used), delivered over HTML5... 

If you ask me, this issue should be closed only when the [new rewrite](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/1436#issuecomment-357651466) of the plugin shows up, an updated version that deals with the API changes gradually put in place by the ITVhub people... So, kindly reconsider and reopen, thanks!

Just a stupid question, and I have to ask, since you didn't answer the questions of the issue template, are you using Livestreamer by accident?

That non-matching stream quality issue was fixed right after Streamlink was forked from Livestreamer and was caused by Twitch's new stream quality naming scheme which introduced optional fps values in the quality names, which broke the old regexp. Your screenshot looks like it doesn't match qualities with fps values.

These are all commits affecting the regexp since the fork
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/commit/5ef06735ec188db30c3b3b6cae36d3ef5933ee9d#diff-dc12d29f5b1be621df2b857139dbec16R40
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/commit/e1b6677a2a8659c823c1b846980342f0e766f3fc#diff-805dc2fac848cb5f82dd28259090acf1R44
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/commit/09899ee468bb98e3cd87e6904175d1988865259e#diff-805dc2fac848cb5f82dd28259090acf1R47

If you're using Streamlink, the only way I can think of this happening is if you've set the [`stream-sorting-excludes`](https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#cmdoption-stream-sorting-excludes) parameter. If `160p, 360p, 720p, 720p60, 1080p60` are the available streams, `best` should be the alias for `1080p60`, unless you've filtered the list via this parameter.

Anyway, it's interesting to know if this is a breach of the standard or not.  As I read https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5545#section-3.3.6 a single P without any trailing period information is invalid, hence it's not a bug in vobject.

There is always a question if one should create workarounds when software applications break the standard.  I think the most proper would be:

1) try to fix this issue at the producer side, as the producer of the ical data is breaching the standard.
2) if the above fails, make a filter before feeding the data to the caldav library, i.e. a regexp replace s/^DURATION:P$/DURATION:P0S/

Here's an example from ECharts candlestick example at https://ecomfe.github.io/echarts-examples/public/editor.html?c=doc-example/candlestick-axisPointer

The options `series.itemStyle.normal.borderColor` and `series.itemStyle.normal.borderColor0` need to be set to `null` which means no border color as follows:
```
...
itemStyle: {
  normal: {
    color: '#06B800',
    color0: '#FA0000',
    borderColor: null,
    borderColor0: null
  }
}
```
to get something like this:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9349772/44741965-f4c89000-aafe-11e8-83b8-ce49cafe4eee.png)

Setting `borderColor` and `borderColor0` to `None` via `_option` currently lets pyecharts generate `itemStyle` as follows:
```
...
"itemStyle": {
  "normal": {
    "color": "#06BB00",
    "color0": "#FA0000"
    }
}
```
with `borderColor` and `borderColor0` both missing. This generates a chart like this (notice the candlesticks outlines):
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9349772/44742286-c5665300-aaff-11e8-965a-777881aac05c.png)
In this case, the default values of `borderColor` and `borderColor0` give incorrect result and the removed values need to exist to specify that these two options should be `null` meaning there is no outline (contrary to the default).

Also my rational is that if I don't want an option to exist, I wouldn't add it from the beginning. Adding an option with `None` value means I want it to exist.

I think calling `remove_key_with_none_value()` from the `options` property needs to be reconsidered. The current implementation doesn't allow setting any options to `null` via pyecharts. So if later the `borderColor` and `borderColor0` options are implemented, I would still be unable to use them for the above case and would need to keep using the `_option` approach. Probably calling `remove_key_with_none_value()` only when required both by the internal working and by the package users if/when required would be a better approach than calling it from the `options` property. As this would be a big effort, I think implementing my suggestion of a `remove_none_options` option would be a faster solution. I'm in favor of the other suggestion though.

This will not be important for getting the Python interface correctly; it is just a question of aesthetics and consistency.

My main motivation is simply that I don't want to have to remember which of "galsim.sbprofile", "sbp", "SBProfile", etc. is correct in different contexts.  But I'd also like to see the directory explicit in our includes, e.g. ```#include "galsim/sbprofile/SBProfile.h"```; that makes it immediately clear to the reader where a header is coming from (though I'd be content with just one level of names there).

I'd be quite happy to drop the "meas" I proposed putting between "galsim" and "hsm".  And I think it's worth pointing out that within the package, you're practically always be within a ```namespace galsim {}``` block, so you wouldn't need to say ```galsim::``` most of the time.

I'd also like to avoid namespaces that get so long that namespace aliases become the standard way of scoping things - that's been a total disaster in the LSST codebase in my opinion.

I don't know if the narrows down the scope significantly enough for you, but this is what I know so far:

Django's unit tests -- specifically the test_runner tests, call the python executable in a subprocess. The command line that is used specifies warnings options, and looks something like this:

    /Users/ian/.virtualenvs/py3k_django/bin/python3.2 -Wignore:::django.utils.translation "/Users/ian/Code/Frameworks/py3k_django/3k/django/bin/django-admin.py" test --settings=regressiontests.settings --option_b foo

Python 3.2.2, under virtualenv, raises an ImportError when the -W flag is passed, indicating that it can't load the tokenize module. (The python interpreter continues to run after this, but the import error itself ends up on the output stream, and the Django unit tests fail for that reason)

When loading the same version of python, with or without the -W flag, the tokenize module is available for import as soon as the interpreter prompt appears.

From dtruss logs, I can see that python is checking in these locations for the tokenize module when the -W flag is passed:

Another thing that's different about the czmq reactor (and one of the reasons it passes stream) is that it does not call recv itself, but rather passes `stream, event_mask`, and application code calls recv as appropriate.  If we did it this way, the obvious name would be `on_pollin`.
